The anterior section faces west-southwest, but part of it was removed to accommodate the construction of the [[Ryōmō Line]]. A Fujisan Shrine (dedicated to the deity of Mount Fuji) sits atop the posterior section, from which the kofun derives its name. Artifacts suggest the mound was constructed in the mid-5th century. Its burial facility features a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a hallmark of royal tombs from the Kinki region during the same period. The site was designated a city historic site in 1966 and the sarcophagus was classified as a Prefectural Important Cultural Property in 2001. == Structure == The dimensions of the kofun are as followsDimensions based on the explanation board at the site.: * Total length: 125 meters * '''Anterior section''' ''' Width: 83.2 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters ''' Some sections removed for the Ryōmō Line construction. * '''Posterior section''' ''' Diameter: 77.2 meters ''' Height: 9.5 meters The mound's orientation is approximately 74° east of north. It features a three-tiered structure covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and lined with large cylindrical haniwa. Surrounding the mound is a shield-shaped moat, which extends the total length of the site to 192 meters. == Artifacts == === Nagamochi-style Sarcophagus === [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun sekkan.JPG|thumb|240px|right|{{center|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important Cultural Property)}}]] [[File:お富士山古墳石棺（復元）.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Replica of the sarcophagus}}{{small|Displayed at the National Museum of Japanese History.}}]] The [[Nagamochi-style sarcophagus]] from Ofujiyama Kofun is preserved near the shrine at the posterior section. A rounded, domed lid rests on top. This sarcophagus type is characteristic of royal tombs in the Kinki region during the 5th century. In Gunma Prefecture, only the [[Ota Tenjinyama Kofun]] shares this feature. Few examples are found elsewhere in the Kanto regionOther examples include Takayanagi Chōshizuka Kofun and San-no-Funeme Ōtsukayama Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.. The craftsmanship and materials suggest that artisans from the Kinki region constructed the sarcophagus locally, underscoring strong ties with the Kinki region. Foreground: Ryōmō Line tracks.}} }} [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun zenkei-1.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Panoramic view from the bridge}}{{small|Left: anterior section; right: posterior section.}}]] [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun funcho.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Posterior section}}{{small|Fujisan Shrine and sarcophagus (far right).}}]] '''Ofujiyama Kofun''' (お富士山古墳), also referred to as御富士山古墳, is a keyhole-shaped burial mound located in [[Isesaki, Gunma|Isesaki City]], [[Gunma Prefecture]]. It has been designated as a historic site by Isesaki City, and its Nagamochi-style sarcophagus is recognized as a Prefectural Important Cultural Property. The mound, built during the mid-5th century, is notable for its use of a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a characteristic feature of royal tombs in the Kinki region. == Overview == Situated in the northwestern part of Isesaki City, Ofujiyama Kofun is the largest keyhole-shaped kofun in the city. The anterior section faces west-southwest, but part of it was removed to accommodate the construction of the [[Ryōmō Line]]. A Fujisan Shrine (dedicated to the deity of Mount Fuji) sits atop the posterior section, from which the kofun derives its name. Artifacts suggest the mound was constructed in the mid-5th century. Its burial facility features a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a hallmark of royal tombs from the Kinki region during the same period. The site was designated a city historic site in 1966 and the sarcophagus was classified as a Prefectural Important Cultural Property in 2001. == Structure == The dimensions of the kofun are as followsDimensions based on the explanation board at the site.: * Total length: 125 meters * '''Anterior section''' ''' Width: 83.2 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters ''' Some sections removed for the Ryōmō Line construction. * '''Posterior section''' ''' Diameter: 77.2 meters ''' Height: 9.5 meters The mound's orientation is approximately 74° east of north. It features a three-tiered structure covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and lined with large cylindrical haniwa. Surrounding the mound is a shield-shaped moat, which extends the total length of the site to 192 meters. == Artifacts == === Nagamochi-style Sarcophagus === [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun sekkan.JPG|thumb|240px|right|{{center|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important Cultural Property)}}]] [[File:お富士山古墳石棺（復元）.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Replica of the sarcophagus}}{{small|Displayed at the National Museum of Japanese History.}}]] The [[Nagamochi-style sarcophagus]] from Ofujiyama Kofun is preserved near the shrine at the posterior section. It is composed of six sandstone slabs: * Total length: 285 cm * Width: 121 cm * Weight: 6.8 tons The sarcophagus features a rectangular base formed by a floor slab, two side slabs, and two end slabs. A rounded, domed lid rests on top. This sarcophagus type is characteristic of royal tombs in the Kinki region during the 5th century. In Gunma Prefecture, only the [[Ota Tenjinyama Kofun]] shares this feature. Few examples are found elsewhere in the Kanto regionOther examples include Takayanagi Chōshizuka Kofun and San-no-Funeme Ōtsukayama Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.. The craftsmanship and materials suggest that artisans from the Kinki region constructed the sarcophagus locally, underscoring strong ties with the Kinki region. Historical records such as the Edo-period "Hatsuhunreki" and "Isesaki Fudoki" also document the sarcophagus. A replica is displayed at the Gunma Prefectural Museum of History. === Other Artifacts === Artifacts include imitation stone knives made from steatite. File:お富士山古墳 出土品.JPG|Artifacts

Surrounding the mound is a shield-shaped moat, which extends the total length of the site to 192 meters. == Artifacts == === Nagamochi-style Sarcophagus === [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun sekkan.JPG|thumb|240px|right|{{center|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important Cultural Property)}}]] [[File:お富士山古墳石棺（復元）.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Replica of the sarcophagus}}{{small|Displayed at the National Museum of Japanese History.}}]] The [[Nagamochi-style sarcophagus]] from Ofujiyama Kofun is preserved near the shrine at the posterior section. It is composed of six sandstone slabs: * Total length: 285 cm * Width: 121 cm * Weight: 6.8 tons The sarcophagus features a rectangular base formed by a floor slab, two side slabs, and two end slabs. A rounded, domed lid rests on top. This sarcophagus type is characteristic of royal tombs in the Kinki region during the 5th century. In Gunma Prefecture, only the [[Ota Tenjinyama Kofun]] shares this feature. Few examples are found elsewhere in the Kanto regionOther examples include Takayanagi Chōshizuka Kofun and San-no-Funeme Ōtsukayama Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.. The craftsmanship and materials suggest that artisans from the Kinki region constructed the sarcophagus locally, underscoring strong ties with the Kinki region. Historical records such as the Edo-period "Hatsuhunreki" and "Isesaki Fudoki" also document the sarcophagus. A replica is displayed at the Gunma Prefectural Museum of History. === Other Artifacts === Artifacts include imitation stone knives made from steatite. File:お富士山古墳 出土品.JPG|Artifacts

{{Infobox ancient site |name=Ofujiyama Kofun |location=[[Isesaki, Gunma|Isesaki]], [[Gunma Prefecture]] |coordinates={{coord|36|20|16.93|N|139|10|19.60|E|region:JP-10_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ofujiyama Kofun}} |shape=[[Keyhole-shaped burial mound|Keyhole-shaped]] |construction_period=Mid-5th century |burial=Nagamochi-style sarcophagus |length=125 meters |heritage=Isesaki City Designated Historic Site |cultural_property=Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important Cultural Property) |artifacts=[[Cylindrical haniwa|Cylindrical haniwa]] |image=[[File:Ofujiyama-kofun zenkei-2.JPG|300px]]
Panoramic view
{{small|Right: anterior section; left: posterior section. Foreground: Ryōmō Line tracks.}} }} [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun zenkei-1.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Panoramic view from the bridge}}{{small|Left: anterior section; right: posterior section.}}]] [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun funcho.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Posterior section}}{{small|Fujisan Shrine and sarcophagus (far right).}}]] '''Ofujiyama Kofun''' (お富士山古墳), also referred to as御富士山古墳, is a keyhole-shaped burial mound located in [[Isesaki, Gunma|Isesaki City]], [[Gunma Prefecture]]. It has been designated as a historic site by Isesaki City, and its Nagamochi-style sarcophagus is recognized as a Prefectural Important Cultural Property. The mound, built during the mid-5th century, is notable for its use of a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a characteristic feature of royal tombs in the Kinki region. == Overview == Situated in the northwestern part of Isesaki City, Ofujiyama Kofun is the largest keyhole-shaped kofun in the city. The anterior section faces west-southwest, but part of it was removed to accommodate the construction of the [[Ryōmō Line]]. A Fujisan Shrine (dedicated to the deity of Mount Fuji) sits atop the posterior section, from which the kofun derives its name. Artifacts suggest the mound was constructed in the mid-5th century. Its burial facility features a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a hallmark of royal tombs from the Kinki region during the same period. The site was designated a city historic site in 1966 and the sarcophagus was classified as a Prefectural Important Cultural Property in 2001. == Structure == The dimensions of the kofun are as followsDimensions based on the explanation board at the site.: * Total length: 125 meters * '''Anterior section''' ''' Width: 83.2 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters ''' Some sections removed for the Ryōmō Line construction. * '''Posterior section''' ''' Diameter: 77.2 meters ''' Height: 9.5 meters The mound's orientation is approximately 74° east of north. It features a three-tiered structure covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and lined with large cylindrical haniwa. Surrounding the mound is a shield-shaped moat, which extends the total length of the site to 192 meters. == Artifacts == === Nagamochi-style Sarcophagus === [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun sekkan.JPG|thumb|240px|right|{{center|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important Cultural Property)}}]] [[File:お富士山古墳石棺（復元）.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Replica of the sarcophagus}}{{small|Displayed at the National Museum of Japanese History.}}]] The [[Nagamochi-style sarcophagus]] from Ofujiyama Kofun is preserved near the shrine at the posterior section. It is composed of six sandstone slabs: * Total length: 285 cm * Width: 121 cm * Weight: 6.8 tons The sarcophagus features a rectangular base formed by a floor slab, two side slabs, and two end slabs. A rounded, domed lid rests on top. This sarcophagus type is characteristic of royal tombs in the Kinki region during the 5th century. In Gunma Prefecture, only the [[Ota Tenjinyama Kofun]] shares this feature. Few examples are found elsewhere in the Kanto regionOther examples include Takayanagi Chōshizuka Kofun and San-no-Funeme Ōtsukayama Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.. The craftsmanship and materials suggest that artisans from the Kinki region constructed the sarcophagus locally, underscoring strong ties with the Kinki region. Historical records such as the Edo-period "Hatsuhunreki" and "Isesaki Fudoki" also document the sarcophagus. A replica is displayed at the Gunma Prefectural Museum of History. === Other Artifacts === Artifacts include imitation stone knives made from steatite. File:お富士山古墳 出土品.JPG|Artifacts

The site was designated a city historic site in 1966 and the sarcophagus was classified as a Prefectural Important Cultural Property in 2001. == Structure == The dimensions of the kofun are as followsDimensions based on the explanation board at the site.: * Total length: 125 meters * '''Anterior section''' ''' Width: 83.2 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters ''' Some sections removed for the Ryōmō Line construction. * '''Posterior section''' ''' Diameter: 77.2 meters ''' Height: 9.5 meters The mound's orientation is approximately 74° east of north. It features a three-tiered structure covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and lined with large cylindrical haniwa. Surrounding the mound is a shield-shaped moat, which extends the total length of the site to 192 meters. == Artifacts == === Nagamochi-style Sarcophagus === [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun sekkan.JPG|thumb|240px|right|{{center|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important Cultural Property)}}]] [[File:お富士山古墳石棺（復元）.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Replica of the sarcophagus}}{{small|Displayed at the National Museum of Japanese History.}}]] The [[Nagamochi-style sarcophagus]] from Ofujiyama Kofun is preserved near the shrine at the posterior section. It is composed of six sandstone slabs: * Total length: 285 cm * Width: 121 cm * Weight: 6.8 tons The sarcophagus features a rectangular base formed by a floor slab, two side slabs, and two end slabs. A rounded, domed lid rests on top. This sarcophagus type is characteristic of royal tombs in the Kinki region during the 5th century. In Gunma Prefecture, only the [[Ota Tenjinyama Kofun]] shares this feature. Few examples are found elsewhere in the Kanto regionOther examples include Takayanagi Chōshizuka Kofun and San-no-Funeme Ōtsukayama Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.. The craftsmanship and materials suggest that artisans from the Kinki region constructed the sarcophagus locally, underscoring strong ties with the Kinki region. Historical records such as the Edo-period "Hatsuhunreki" and "Isesaki Fudoki" also document the sarcophagus. A replica is displayed at the Gunma Prefectural Museum of History. === Other Artifacts === Artifacts include imitation stone knives made from steatite. File:お富士山古墳 出土品.JPG|Artifacts
{{small|Exhibited at the Isesaki City Akabori History and Folklore Museum.}} == Cultural Properties == === Gunma Prefecture === * '''Important Cultural Property (Tangible Cultural Property)''' ''' {{Infobox ancient site |name=Ofujiyama Kofun |location=[[Isesaki, Gunma|Isesaki]], [[Gunma Prefecture]] |coordinates={{coord|36|20|16.93|N|139|10|19.60|E|region:JP-10_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ofujiyama Kofun}} |shape=[[Keyhole-shaped burial mound|Keyhole-shaped]] |construction_period=Mid-5th century |burial=Nagamochi-style sarcophagus |length=125 meters |heritage=Isesaki City Designated Historic Site |cultural_property=Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important Cultural Property) |artifacts=[[Cylindrical haniwa|Cylindrical haniwa]] |image=[[File:Ofujiyama-kofun zenkei-2.JPG|300px]]
Panoramic view
{{small|Right: anterior section; left: posterior section. Foreground: Ryōmō Line tracks.}} }} [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun zenkei-1.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Panoramic view from the bridge}}{{small|Left: anterior section; right: posterior section.}}]] [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun funcho.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Posterior section}}{{small|Fujisan Shrine and sarcophagus (far right).}}]] '''Ofujiyama Kofun''' (お富士山古墳), also referred to as御富士山古墳, is a keyhole-shaped burial mound located in [[Isesaki, Gunma|Isesaki City]], [[Gunma Prefecture]]. It has been designated as a historic site by Isesaki City, and its Nagamochi-style sarcophagus is recognized as a Prefectural Important Cultural Property. The mound, built during the mid-5th century, is notable for its use of a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a characteristic feature of royal tombs in the Kinki region. == Overview == Situated in the northwestern part of Isesaki City, Ofujiyama Kofun is the largest keyhole-shaped kofun in the city. The anterior section faces west-southwest, but part of it was removed to accommodate the construction of the [[Ryōmō Line]]. A Fujisan Shrine (dedicated to the deity of Mount Fuji) sits atop the posterior section, from which the kofun derives its name. Artifacts suggest the mound was constructed in the mid-5th century. Its burial facility features a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a hallmark of royal tombs from the Kinki region during the same period. The site was designated a city historic site in 1966 and the sarcophagus was classified as a Prefectural Important Cultural Property in 2001. == Structure == The dimensions of the kofun are as followsDimensions based on the explanation board at the site.: * Total length: 125 meters * '''Anterior section''' ''' Width: 83.2 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters ''' Some sections removed for the Ryōmō Line construction. * '''Posterior section''' ''' Diameter: 77.2 meters ''' Height: 9.5 meters The mound's orientation is approximately 74° east of north. It features a three-tiered structure covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and lined with large cylindrical haniwa. Surrounding the mound is a shield-shaped moat, which extends the total length of the site to 192 meters. == Artifacts == === Nagamochi-style Sarcophagus === [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun sekkan.JPG|thumb|240px|right|{{center|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important Cultural Property)}}]] [[File:お富士山古墳石棺（復元）.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Replica of the sarcophagus}}{{small|Displayed at the National Museum of Japanese History.}}]] The [[Nagamochi-style sarcophagus]] from Ofujiyama Kofun is preserved near the shrine at the posterior section. It is composed of six sandstone slabs: * Total length: 285 cm * Width: 121 cm * Weight: 6.8 tons The sarcophagus features a rectangular base formed by a floor slab, two side slabs, and two end slabs. A rounded, domed lid rests on top. This sarcophagus type is characteristic of royal tombs in the Kinki region during the 5th century. In Gunma Prefecture, only the [[Ota Tenjinyama Kofun]] shares this feature. Few examples are found elsewhere in the Kanto regionOther examples include Takayanagi Chōshizuka Kofun and San-no-Funeme Ōtsukayama Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.. The craftsmanship and materials suggest that artisans from the Kinki region constructed the sarcophagus locally, underscoring strong ties with the Kinki region. Historical records such as the Edo-period "Hatsuhunreki" and "Isesaki Fudoki" also document the sarcophagus. A replica is displayed at the Gunma Prefectural Museum of History. === Other Artifacts === Artifacts include imitation stone knives made from steatite. File:お富士山古墳 出土品.JPG|Artifacts

The anterior section faces west-southwest, but part of it was removed to accommodate the construction of the [[Ryōmō Line]]. A Fujisan Shrine (dedicated to the deity of Mount Fuji) sits atop the posterior section, from which the kofun derives its name. Artifacts suggest the mound was constructed in the mid-5th century. Its burial facility features a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a hallmark of royal tombs from the Kinki region during the same period. The site was designated a city historic site in 1966 and the sarcophagus was classified as a Prefectural Important Cultural Property in 2001. == Structure == The dimensions of the kofun are as followsDimensions based on the explanation board at the site.: * Total length: 125 meters * '''Anterior section''' ''' Width: 83.2 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters ''' Some sections removed for the Ryōmō Line construction. * '''Posterior section''' ''' Diameter: 77.2 meters ''' Height: 9.5 meters The mound's orientation is approximately 74° east of north. It features a three-tiered structure covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and lined with large cylindrical haniwa. Surrounding the mound is a shield-shaped moat, which extends the total length of the site to 192 meters. == Artifacts == === Nagamochi-style Sarcophagus === [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun sekkan.JPG|thumb|240px|right|{{center|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important Cultural Property)}}]] [[File:お富士山古墳石棺（復元）.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Replica of the sarcophagus}}{{small|Displayed at the National Museum of Japanese History.}}]] The [[Nagamochi-style sarcophagus]] from Ofujiyama Kofun is preserved near the shrine at the posterior section. It is composed of six sandstone slabs: * Total length: 285 cm * Width: 121 cm * Weight: 6.8 tons The sarcophagus features a rectangular base formed by a floor slab, two side slabs, and two end slabs. A rounded, domed lid rests on top. This sarcophagus type is characteristic of royal tombs in the Kinki region during the 5th century. In Gunma Prefecture, only the [[Ota Tenjinyama Kofun]] shares this feature. Few examples are found elsewhere in the Kanto regionOther examples include Takayanagi Chōshizuka Kofun and San-no-Funeme Ōtsukayama Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.. The craftsmanship and materials suggest that artisans from the Kinki region constructed the sarcophagus locally, underscoring strong ties with the Kinki region. Historical records such as the Edo-period "Hatsuhunreki" and "Isesaki Fudoki" also document the sarcophagus. A replica is displayed at the Gunma Prefectural Museum of History. === Other Artifacts === Artifacts include imitation stone knives made from steatite. File:お富士山古墳 出土品.JPG|Artifacts
{{small|Exhibited at the Isesaki City Akabori History and Folklore Museum.}} == Cultural Properties == === Gunma Prefecture === * '''Important Cultural Property (Tangible Cultural Property)''' ''' Few examples are found elsewhere in the Kanto regionOther examples include Takayanagi Chōshizuka Kofun and San-no-Funeme Ōtsukayama Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.. Foreground: Ryōmō Line tracks.}} }} [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun zenkei-1.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Panoramic view from the bridge}}{{small|Left: anterior section; right: posterior section.}}]] [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun funcho.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Posterior section}}{{small|Fujisan Shrine and sarcophagus (far right).}}]] '''Ofujiyama Kofun''' (お富士山古墳), also referred to as御富士山古墳, is a keyhole-shaped burial mound located in [[Isesaki, Gunma|Isesaki City]], [[Gunma Prefecture]]. It has been designated as a historic site by Isesaki City, and its Nagamochi-style sarcophagus is recognized as a Prefectural Important Cultural Property. The mound, built during the mid-5th century, is notable for its use of a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a characteristic feature of royal tombs in the Kinki region. == Overview == Situated in the northwestern part of Isesaki City, Ofujiyama Kofun is the largest keyhole-shaped kofun in the city. The anterior section faces west-southwest, but part of it was removed to accommodate the construction of the [[Ryōmō Line]]. A Fujisan Shrine (dedicated to the deity of Mount Fuji) sits atop the posterior section, from which the kofun derives its name. Artifacts suggest the mound was constructed in the mid-5th century. Its burial facility features a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a hallmark of royal tombs from the Kinki region during the same period. The site was designated a city historic site in 1966 and the sarcophagus was classified as a Prefectural Important Cultural Property in 2001. == Structure == The dimensions of the kofun are as followsDimensions based on the explanation board at the site.: * Total length: 125 meters * '''Anterior section''' ''' Width: 83.2 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters ''' Some sections removed for the Ryōmō Line construction. * '''Posterior section''' ''' Diameter: 77.2 meters ''' Height: 9.5 meters The mound's orientation is approximately 74° east of north. It features a three-tiered structure covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and lined with large cylindrical haniwa. Surrounding the mound is a shield-shaped moat, which extends the total length of the site to 192 meters. == Artifacts == === Nagamochi-style Sarcophagus === [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun sekkan.JPG|thumb|240px|right|{{center|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important Cultural Property)}}]] [[File:お富士山古墳石棺（復元）.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Replica of the sarcophagus}}{{small|Displayed at the National Museum of Japanese History.}}]] The [[Nagamochi-style sarcophagus]] from Ofujiyama Kofun is preserved near the shrine at the posterior section. It is composed of six sandstone slabs: * Total length: 285 cm * Width: 121 cm * Weight: 6.8 tons The sarcophagus features a rectangular base formed by a floor slab, two side slabs, and two end slabs. A rounded, domed lid rests on top. This sarcophagus type is characteristic of royal tombs in the Kinki region during the 5th century. In Gunma Prefecture, only the [[Ota Tenjinyama Kofun]] shares this feature. Few examples are found elsewhere in the Kanto regionOther examples include Takayanagi Chōshizuka Kofun and San-no-Funeme Ōtsukayama Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.. The craftsmanship and materials suggest that artisans from the Kinki region constructed the sarcophagus locally, underscoring strong ties with the Kinki region. Historical records such as the Edo-period "Hatsuhunreki" and "Isesaki Fudoki" also document the sarcophagus. A replica is displayed at the Gunma Prefectural Museum of History. === Other Artifacts === Artifacts include imitation stone knives made from steatite. File:お富士山古墳 出土品.JPG|Artifacts
{{small|Exhibited at the Isesaki City Akabori History and Folklore Museum.}} == Cultural Properties == === Gunma Prefecture === * '''Important Cultural Property (Tangible Cultural Property)''' ''' Nagamochi-style sarcophagus from Ofujiyama Kofun (archaeological material) – Designated March 23, 2001. === Isesaki City === * '''Historic Site''' ''' Ofujiyama Kofun – Designated April 12, 1966. == {{Infobox ancient site |name=Ofujiyama Kofun |location=[[Isesaki, Gunma|Isesaki]], [[Gunma Prefecture]] |coordinates={{coord|36|20|16.93|N|139|10|19.60|E|region:JP-10_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ofujiyama Kofun}} |type=[[Keyhole-shaped burial mound|Keyhole-shaped]] |construction_period=Mid-5th century |burial=Nagamochi-style sarcophagus |length=125 meters |heritage=Isesaki City Designated Historic Site |cultural_property=Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important Cultural Property) |artifacts=[[Cylindrical haniwa|Cylindrical haniwa]] |image=[[File:Ofujiyama-kofun zenkei-2.JPG|300px]]
Panoramic view
{{small|Right: anterior section; left: posterior section. Foreground: Ryōmō Line tracks.}} }} [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun zenkei-1.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Panoramic view from the bridge}}{{small|Left: anterior section; right: posterior section.}}]] [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun funcho.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Posterior section}}{{small|Fujisan Shrine and sarcophagus (far right).}}]] '''Ofujiyama Kofun''' (お富士山古墳), also referred to as御富士山古墳, is a keyhole-shaped burial mound located in [[Isesaki, Gunma|Isesaki City]], [[Gunma Prefecture]]. It has been designated as a historic site by Isesaki City, and its Nagamochi-style sarcophagus is recognized as a Prefectural Important Cultural Property. The mound, built during the mid-5th century, is notable for its use of a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a characteristic feature of royal tombs in the Kinki region. == Overview == Situated in the northwestern part of Isesaki City, Ofujiyama Kofun is the largest keyhole-shaped kofun in the city. The anterior section faces west-southwest, but part of it was removed to accommodate the construction of the [[Ryōmō Line]]. A Fujisan Shrine (dedicated to the deity of Mount Fuji) sits atop the posterior section, from which the kofun derives its name. Artifacts suggest the mound was constructed in the mid-5th century. Its burial facility features a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a hallmark of royal tombs from the Kinki region during the same period. The site was designated a city historic site in 1966 and the sarcophagus was classified as a Prefectural Important Cultural Property in 2001. == Structure == The dimensions of the kofun are as followsDimensions based on the explanation board at the site.: * Total length: 125 meters * '''Anterior section''' ''' Width: 83.2 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters ''' Some sections removed for the Ryōmō Line construction. * '''Posterior section''' ''' Diameter: 77.2 meters ''' Height: 9.5 meters The mound's orientation is approximately 74° east of north. It features a three-tiered structure covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and lined with large cylindrical haniwa. Surrounding the mound is a shield-shaped moat, which extends the total length of the site to 192 meters. == Artifacts == === Nagamochi-style Sarcophagus === [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun sekkan.JPG|thumb|240px|right|{{center|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important Cultural Property)}}]] [[File:お富士山古墳石棺（復元）.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Replica of the sarcophagus}}{{small|Displayed at the National Museum of Japanese History.}}]] The [[Nagamochi-style sarcophagus]] from Ofujiyama Kofun is preserved near the shrine at the posterior section. It is composed of six sandstone slabs: * Total length: 285 cm * Width: 121 cm * Weight: 6.8 tons The sarcophagus features a rectangular base formed by a floor slab, two side slabs, and two end slabs. A rounded, domed lid rests on top. This sarcophagus type is characteristic of royal tombs in the Kinki region during the 5th century. In Gunma Prefecture, only the [[Ota Tenjinyama Kofun]] shares this feature. Few examples are found elsewhere in the Kanto regionOther examples include Takayanagi Chōshizuka Kofun and San-no-Funeme Ōtsukayama Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.. The craftsmanship and materials suggest that artisans from the Kinki region constructed the sarcophagus locally, underscoring strong ties with the Kinki region. Historical records such as the Edo-period "Hatsuhunreki" and "Isesaki Fudoki" also document the sarcophagus. A replica is displayed at the Gunma Prefectural Museum of History. === Other Artifacts === Artifacts include imitation stone knives made from steatite. File:お富士山古墳 出土品.JPG|Artifacts
{{small|Exhibited at the Isesaki City Akabori History and Folklore Museum.}} == Cultural Properties == === Gunma Prefecture === * '''Important Cultural Property (Tangible Cultural Property)''' ''' Nagamochi-style sarcophagus from Ofujiyama Kofun (archaeological material) – Designated March 23, 2001. === Isesaki City === * '''Historic Site''' ''' Ofujiyama Kofun – Designated April 12, 1966. == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Explanation Board at the Site (Isesaki City Board of Education, installed February 5, 1999) * Sarcophagus Preservation Facility Documentation == Related Sites == * [[Keno Province|Keno]] * [[Ōta Tenjinyama Kofun|Ota Tenjinyama Kofun]] – The largest kofun in eastern Japan, also featuring a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus. == External Links == revision.text:
{{Infobox ancient site |name=Ofujiyama Kofun |location=[[Isesaki, Gunma|Isesaki]], [[Gunma Prefecture]] |coordinates={{coord|36|20|16.93|N|139|10|19.60|E|region:JP-10_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ofujiyama Kofun}} |type=[[Keyhole-shaped burial mound|Keyhole-shaped]] |construction_period=Mid-5th century |burial=Nagamochi-style sarcophagus |length=125 meters |heritage=Isesaki City Designated Historic Site |cultural_property=Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important Cultural Property) |artifacts=[[Cylindrical haniwa|Cylindrical haniwa]] |image=[[File:Ofujiyama-kofun zenkei-2.JPG|300px]]
Panoramic view
{{small|Right: anterior section; left: posterior section. Foreground: Ryōmō Line tracks.}} }} [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun zenkei-1.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Panoramic view from the bridge}}{{small|Left: anterior section; right: posterior section.}}]] [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun funcho.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Posterior section}}{{small|Fujisan Shrine and sarcophagus (far right).}}]] '''Ofujiyama Kofun''' (お富士山古墳), also referred to as御富士山古墳, is a keyhole-shaped burial mound located in [[Isesaki, Gunma|Isesaki City]], [[Gunma Prefecture]]. It has been designated as a historic site by Isesaki City, and its Nagamochi-style sarcophagus is recognized as a Prefectural Important Cultural Property. The mound, built during the mid-5th century, is notable for its use of a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a characteristic feature of royal tombs in the Kinki region. == Overview == Situated in the northwestern part of Isesaki City, Ofujiyama Kofun is the largest keyhole-shaped kofun in the city. The anterior section faces west-southwest, but part of it was removed to accommodate the construction of the [[Ryōmō Line]]. A Fujisan Shrine (dedicated to the deity of Mount Fuji) sits atop the posterior section, from which the kofun derives its name. Artifacts suggest the mound was constructed in the mid-5th century. Its burial facility features a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a hallmark of royal tombs from the Kinki region during the same period. The site was designated a city historic site in 1966 and the sarcophagus was classified as a Prefectural Important Cultural Property in 2001. == Structure == The dimensions of the kofun are as followsDimensions based on the explanation board at the site.: * Total length: 125 meters * '''Anterior section''' ''' Width: 83.2 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters ''' Some sections removed for the Ryōmō Line construction. * '''Posterior section''' ''' Diameter: 77.2 meters ''' Height: 9.5 meters The mound's orientation is approximately 74° east of north. It features a three-tiered structure covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and lined with large cylindrical haniwa. Surrounding the mound is a shield-shaped moat, which extends the total length of the site to 192 meters. == Artifacts == === Nagamochi-style Sarcophagus === [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun sekkan.JPG|thumb|240px|right|{{center|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important Cultural Property)}}]] [[File:お富士山古墳石棺（復元）.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Replica of the sarcophagus}}{{small|Displayed at the National Museum of Japanese History.}}]] The [[Nagamochi-style sarcophagus]] from Ofujiyama Kofun is preserved near the shrine at the posterior section. It is composed of six sandstone slabs: * Total length: 285 cm * Width: 121 cm * Weight: 6.8 tons The sarcophagus features a rectangular base formed by a floor slab, two side slabs, and two end slabs. A rounded, domed lid rests on top. This sarcophagus type is characteristic of royal tombs in the Kinki region during the 5th century. In Gunma Prefecture, only the [[Ota Tenjinyama Kofun]] shares this feature. Few examples are found elsewhere in the Kanto regionOther examples include Takayanagi Chōshizuka Kofun and San-no-Funeme Ōtsukayama Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.. The craftsmanship and materials suggest that artisans from the Kinki region constructed the sarcophagus locally, underscoring strong ties with the Kinki region. Historical records such as the Edo-period "Hatsuhunreki" and "Isesaki Fudoki" also document the sarcophagus. A replica is displayed at the Gunma Prefectural Museum of History. === Other Artifacts === Artifacts include imitation stone knives made from steatite. File:お富士山古墳 出土品.JPG|Artifacts
{{small|Exhibited at the Isesaki City Akabori History and Folklore Museum.}} == Cultural Properties == === Gunma Prefecture === * '''Important Cultural Property (Tangible Cultural Property)''' ''' Nagamochi-style sarcophagus from Ofujiyama Kofun (archaeological material) – Designated March 23, 2001. === Isesaki City === * '''Historic Site''' ''' Ofujiyama Kofun – Designated April 12, 1966. == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Explanation Board at the Site (Isesaki City Board of Education, installed February 5, 1999) * Sarcophagus Preservation Facility Documentation == Related Sites == * [[Keno Province|Keno]] * [[Ōta Tenjinyama Kofun|Ota Tenjinyama Kofun]] – The largest kofun in eastern Japan, also featuring a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus. == External Links == It features a three-tiered structure covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and lined with large cylindrical haniwa. Surrounding the mound is a shield-shaped moat, which extends the total length of the site to 192 meters. == Artifacts == === Nagamochi-style Sarcophagus === [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun sekkan.JPG|thumb|240px|right|{{center|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important Cultural Property)}}]] [[File:お富士山古墳石棺（復元）.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Replica of the sarcophagus}}{{small|Displayed at the National Museum of Japanese History.}}]] The [[Nagamochi-style sarcophagus]] from Ofujiyama Kofun is preserved near the shrine at the posterior section. It is composed of six sandstone slabs: * Total length: 285 cm * Width: 121 cm * Weight: 6.8 tons The sarcophagus features a rectangular base formed by a floor slab, two side slabs, and two end slabs. A rounded, domed lid rests on top. This sarcophagus type is characteristic of royal tombs in the Kinki region during the 5th century. In Gunma Prefecture, only the [[Ota Tenjinyama Kofun]] shares this feature. Few examples are found elsewhere in the Kanto regionOther examples include Takayanagi Chōshizuka Kofun and San-no-Funeme Ōtsukayama Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.. The craftsmanship and materials suggest that artisans from the Kinki region constructed the sarcophagus locally, underscoring strong ties with the Kinki region. Historical records such as the Edo-period "Hatsuhunreki" and "Isesaki Fudoki" also document the sarcophagus. A replica is displayed at the Gunma Prefectural Museum of History. === Other Artifacts === Artifacts include imitation stone knives made from steatite. File:お富士山古墳 出土品.JPG|Artifacts

{{Infobox ancient site |name=Ofujiyama Kofun |location=[[Isesaki, Gunma|Isesaki]], [[Gunma Prefecture]] |coordinates={{coord|36|20|16.93|N|139|10|19.60|E|region:JP-10_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ofujiyama Kofun}} |type=[[Keyhole-shaped burial mound|Keyhole-shaped]] |construction_period=Mid-5th century |burial=Nagamochi-style sarcophagus |length=125 meters |heritage=Isesaki City Designated Historic Site |cultural_property=Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important Cultural Property) |artifacts=[[Cylindrical haniwa|Cylindrical haniwa]] |image=[[File:Ofujiyama-kofun zenkei-2.JPG|300px]]
Panoramic view
{{small|Right: anterior section; left: posterior section. Foreground: Ryōmō Line tracks.}} }} [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun zenkei-1.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Panoramic view from the bridge}}{{small|Left: anterior section; right: posterior section.}}]] [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun funcho.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Posterior section}}{{small|Fujisan Shrine and sarcophagus (far right).}}]] '''Ofujiyama Kofun''' (お富士山古墳), also referred to as御富士山古墳, is a keyhole-shaped burial mound located in [[Isesaki, Gunma|Isesaki City]], [[Gunma Prefecture]]. It has been designated as a historic site by Isesaki City, and its Nagamochi-style sarcophagus is recognized as a Prefectural Important Cultural Property. The mound, built during the mid-5th century, is notable for its use of a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a characteristic feature of royal tombs in the Kinki region. == Overview == Situated in the northwestern part of Isesaki City, Ofujiyama Kofun is the largest keyhole-shaped kofun in the city. The anterior section faces west-southwest, but part of it was removed to accommodate the construction of the [[Ryōmō Line]]. A Fujisan Shrine (dedicated to the deity of Mount Fuji) sits atop the posterior section, from which the kofun derives its name. Artifacts suggest the mound was constructed in the mid-5th century. Its burial facility features a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a hallmark of royal tombs from the Kinki region during the same period. The site was designated a city historic site in 1966 and the sarcophagus was classified as a Prefectural Important Cultural Property in 2001. == Structure == The dimensions of the kofun are as followsDimensions based on the explanation board at the site.: * Total length: 125 meters * '''Anterior section''' ''' Width: 83.2 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters ''' Some sections removed for the Ryōmō Line construction. * '''Posterior section''' ''' Diameter: 77.2 meters ''' Height: 9.5 meters The mound's orientation is aqqroximately 74° east of north. It features a three-tiered structure covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and lined with large cylindrical haniwa. Surrounding the mound is a shield-shaped moat, which extends the total length of the site to 192 meters. == Artifacts == === Nagamochi-style Sarcophagus === [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun sekkan.JPG|thumb|240px|right|{{center|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important Cultural Property)}}]] [[File:お富士山古墳石棺（復元）.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Replica of the sarcophagus}}{{small|Displayed at the National Museum of Japanese History.}}]] The [[Nagamochi-style sarcophagus]] from Ofujiyama Kofun is preserved near the shrine at the posterior section. It is composed of six sandstone slabs: * Total length: 285 cm * Width: 121 cm * Weight: 6.8 tons The sarcophagus features a rectangular base formed by a floor slab, two side slabs, and two end slabs. A rounded, domed lid rests on top. This sarcophagus type is characteristic of royal tombs in the Kinki region during the 5th century. In Gunma Prefecture, only the [[Ota Tenjinyama Kofun]] shares this feature. Few examples are found elsewhere in the Kanto regionOther examples include Takayanagi Chōshizuka Kofun and San-no-Funeme Ōtsukayama Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.. The craftsmanship and materials suggest that artisans from the Kinki region constructed the sarcophagus locally, underscoring strong ties with the Kinki region. Historical records such as the Edo-period "Hatsuhunreki" and "Isesaki Fudoki" also document the sarcophagus. A replica is displayed at the Gunma Prefectural Museum of History. === Other Artifacts === Artifacts include imitation stone knives made from steatite. File:お富士山古墳 出土品.JPG|Artifacts

{{Infobox ancient site |name=Ofujiyama Kofun |location=[[Isesaki, Gunma|Isesaki]], [[Gunma Prefecture]] |coordinates={{coord|36|20|16.93|N|139|10|19.60|E|region:JP-10_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ofujiyama Kofun}} |type=[[Keyhole-shaped burial mound|Keyhole-shaped]] |construction_period=Mid-5th century |burial=Nagamochi-style sarcophagus |length=125 meters |heritage=Isesaki City Designated Historic Site |cultural_property=Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important Cultural Property) |artifacts=[[Cylindrical haniwa|Cylindrical haniwa]] |image=[[File:Ofujiyama-kofun zenkei-2.JPG|300px]]
Panoramic view
{{small|Right: anterior section; left: posterior section. Foreground: Ryōmō Line tracks.}} }} [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun zenkei-1.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Panoramic view from the bridge}}{{small|Left: anterior section; right: posterior section.}}]] [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun funcho.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Posterior section}}{{small|Fujisan Shrine and sarcophagus (far right).}}]] '''Ofujiyama Kofun''' (お富士山古墳), also referred to as御富士山古墳, is a keyhole-shaped burial mound located in [[Isesaki, Gunma|Isesaki City]], [[Gunma Prefecture]]. It has been designated as a historic site by Isesaki City, and its Nagamochi-style sarcophagus is recognized as a Prefectural Important Cultural Property. The mound, built during the mid-5th century, is notable for its use of a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a characteristic feature of royal tombs in the Kinki region. == Overview == Situated in the northwestern part of Isesaki City, Ofujiyama Kofun is the largest keyhole-shaped kofun in the city. The anterior section faces west-southwest, but part of it was removed to accommodate the construction of the [[Ryōmō Line]]. A Fujisan Shrine (dedicated to the deity of Mount Fuji) sits atop the posterior section, from which the kofun derives its name. Artifacts suggest the mound was constructed in the mid-5th century. Its burial facility features a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a hallmark of royal tombs from the Kinki region during the same period. The site was designated a city historic site in 1966 and the sarcophagus was classified as a Prefectural Important Cultural Property in 2001. == Structure == The dimensions of the kofun are as followsDimensions based on the explanation board at the site.: * Total length: 125 meters * '''Anterior section''' ''' Width: 83.2 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters ''' Some sections removed for the Ryōmō Line construction. * '''Posterior section''' ''' Diameter: 77.2 meters ''' Height: 9.5 meters The mound's orientation is aqqroximately 74° east of north. It features a three-tiered structure covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and lined with large cylindrical haniwa. Surrounding the mound is a shield-shaped moat, which extends the total length of the site to 192 meters. == Artifacts == === Nagamochi-style Sarcophagus === [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun sekkan.JPG|thumb|240px|right|{{center|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important Cultural Property)}}]] [[File:お富士山古墳石棺（復元）.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Replica of the sarcophagus}}{{small|Displayed at the National Museum of Japanese History.}}]] The [[Nagamochi-style sarcophagus]] from Ofujiyama Kofun is preserved near the shrine at the posterior section. It is composed of six sandstone slabs: * Total length: 285 cm * Width: 121 cm * Weight: 6.8 tons The sarcophagus features a rectangular base formed by a floor slab, two side slabs, and two end slabs. A rounded, domed lid rests on top. This sarcophagus type is characteristic of royal tombs in the Kinki region during the 5th century. In Gunma Prefecture, only the [[Ota Tenjinyama Kofun]] shares this feature. Few examples are found elsewhere in the Kanto regionOther examples include Takayanagi Chōshizuka Kofun and San-no-Funeme Ōtsukayama Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.. The craftsmanship and materials suggest that artisans from the Kinki region constructed the sarcophagus locally, underscoring strong ties with the Kinki region. Historical records such as the Edo-period "Hatsuhunreki" and "Isesaki Fudoki" also document the sarcophagus. A replica is displayed at the Gunma Prefectural Museum of History. === Other Artifacts === Artifacts include imitation stone knives made from steatite. File:お富士山古墳 出土品.JPG|Artifacts

{{Infobox ancient site |name=Ofujiyama Kofun |location=[[Isesaki, Gunma|Isesaki]], [[Gunma Prefecture]] |coordinates={{coord|36|20|16.93|N|139|10|19.60|E|region:JP-10_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ofujiyama Kofun}} |type=[[Keyhole-shaped burial mound|Keyhole-shaped]] |construction_period=Mid-5th century |burial=Nagamochi-style sarcophagus |length=125 meters |heritage=Isesaki City Designated Historic Site |cultural_property=Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important Cultural Property) |artifacts=[[Cylindrical haniwa|Cylindrical haniwa]] |image=[[File:Ofujiyama-kofun zenkei-2.JPG|300px]]
Panoramic view
{{small|Right: anterior section; left: posterior section. Foreground: Ryōmō Line tracks.}} }} [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun zenkei-1.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Panoramic view from the bridge}}{{small|Left: anterior section; right: posterior section.}}]] [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun funcho.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Posterior section}}{{small|Fujisan Shrine and sarcophagus (far right).}}]] '''Ofujiyama Kofun''' (お富士山古墳), also referred to as御富士山古墳, is a keyhole-shaped burial mound located in [[Isesaki, Gunma|Isesaki City]], [[Gunma Prefecture]]. It has been designated as a historic site by Isesaki City, and its Nagamochi-style sarcophagus is recognized as a Prefectural Important Cultural Property. The mound, built during the mid-5th century, is notable for its use of a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a characteristic feature of royal tombs in the Kinki region. == Overview == Situated in the northwestern part of Isesaki City, Ofujiyama Kofun is the largest keyhole-shaped kofun in the city. The anterior section faces west-southwest, but part of it was removed to accommodate the construction of the [[Ryōmō Line]]. A Fujisan Shrine (dedicated to the deity of Mount Fuji) sits atop the posterior section, from which the kofun derives its name. Artifacts suggest the mound was constructed in the mid-5th century. Its burial facility features a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a hallmark of royal tombs from the Kinki region during the same period. The site was designated a city historic site in 1966 and the sarcophagus was classified as a Prefectural Important Cultural Property in 2001. == Structure == The dimensions of the kofun are as followsDimensions based on the explanation board at the site.: * Total length: 125 meters * '''Anterior section''' ''' Width: 83.2 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters ''' Some sections removed for the Ryōmō Line construction. * '''Posterior section''' ''' Diameter: 77.2 meters ''' Height: 9.5 meters The mound's orientation is aqqroximately 74° east of north. It features a three-tiered structure covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and lined with large cylindrical haniwa. Surrounding the mound is a shield-shaped moat, which extends the total length of the site to 192 meters. == Artifacts == === Nagamochi-style Sarcophagus === [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun sekkan.JPG|thumb|240px|right|{{center|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important Cultural Property)}}]] [[File:お富士山古墳石棺（復元）.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Replica of the sarcophagus}}{{small|Displayed at the National Museum of Japanese History.}}]] The [[Nagamochi-style sarcophagus]] from Ofujiyama Kofun is preserved near the shrine at the posterior section. It is composed of six sandstone slabs: * Total length: 285 cm * Width: 121 cm * Weight: 6.8 tons The sarcophagus features a rectangular base formed by a floor slab, two side slabs, and two end slabs. A rounded, domed lid rests on top. This sarcophagus type is characteristic of royal tombs in the Kinki region during the 5th century. In Gunma Prefecture, only the [[Ota Tenjinyama Kofun]] shares this feature. Few examples are found elsewhere in the Kanto regionOther examples include Takayanagi Chōshizuka Kofun and San-no-Funeme Ōtsukayama Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.. The craftsmanship and materials suggest that artisans from the Kinki region constructed the sarcophagus locally, underscoring strong ties with the Kinki region. Historical records such as the Edo-period "Hatsuhunreki" and "Isesaki Fudoki" also document the sarcophagus. A replica is displayed at the Gunma Prefectural Museum of History. === Other Artifacts === Artifacts include imitation stone knives made from steatite. File:お富士山古墳 出土品.JPG|Artifacts
{{small|Exhibited at the Isesaki City Akabori History and Folklore Museum.}} == Cultural Properties == === Gunma Prefecture === * '''Important Cultural Property (Tangible Cultural Property)''' ''' {{Infobox ancient site |name=Ofujiyama Kofun |location=[[Isesaki, Gunma|Isesaki]], [[Gunma Prefecture]] |coordinates={{coord|36|20|16.93|N|139|10|19.60|E|region:JP-10_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ofujiyama Kofun}} |type=[[Keyhole-shaped Kofun|Keyhole-shaped]] |construction_period=Mid-5th century |burial=Nagamochi-style sarcophagus |length=125 meters |heritage=Isesaki City Designated Historic Site |cultural_property=Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important Cultural Property) |artifacts=[[Cylindrical haniwa|Cylindrical haniwa]] |image=[[File:Ofujiyama-kofun zenkei-2.JPG|300px]]
Panoramic view
{{small|Right: anterior section; left: posterior section. Foreground: Ryōmō Line tracks.}} }} [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun zenkei-1.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Panoramic view from the bridge}}{{small|Left: anterior section; right: posterior section.}}]] [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun funcho.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Posterior section}}{{small|Fujisan Shrine and sarcophagus (far right).}}]] '''Ofujiyama Kofun''' (お富士山古墳), also referred to as御富士山古墳, is a keyhole-shaped Kofun located in [[Isesaki, Gunma|Isesaki City]], [[Gunma Prefecture]]. It has been designated as a historic site by Isesaki City, and its Nagamochi-style sarcophagus is recognized as a Prefectural Important Cultural Property. The mound, built during the mid-5th century, is notable for its use of a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a characteristic feature of royal tombs in the Kinki region. == Overview == Situated in the northwestern part of Isesaki City, Ofujiyama Kofun is the largest keyhole-shaped kofun in the city. The anterior section faces west-southwest, but part of it was removed to accommodate the construction of the [[Ryōmō Line]]. A Fujisan Shrine (dedicated to the deity of Mount Fuji) sits atop the posterior section, from which the kofun derives its name. Artifacts suggest the mound was constructed in the mid-5th century. Its burial facility features a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a hallmark of royal tombs from the Kinki region during the same period. The site was designated a city historic site in 1966 and the sarcophagus was classified as a Prefectural Important Cultural Property in 2001. == Structure == The dimensions of the kofun are as followsDimensions based on the explanation board at the site.: * Total length: 125 meters * '''Anterior section''' ''' Width: 83.2 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters ''' Some sections removed for the Ryōmō Line construction. * '''Posterior section''' ''' Diameter: 77.2 meters ''' Height: 9.5 meters The mound's orientation is aqqroximately 74° east of north. It features a three-tiered structure covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and lined with large cylindrical haniwa. Surrounding the mound is a shield-shaped moat, which extends the total length of the site to 192 meters. == Artifacts == === Nagamochi-style Sarcophagus === [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun sekkan.JPG|thumb|240px|right|{{center|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important Cultural Property)}}]] [[File:お富士山古墳石棺（復元）.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Replica of the sarcophagus}}{{small|Displayed at the National Museum of Japanese History.}}]] The [[Nagamochi-style sarcophagus]] from Ofujiyama Kofun is preserved near the shrine at the posterior section. It is composed of six sandstone slabs: * Total length: 285 cm * Width: 121 cm * Weight: 6.8 tons The sarcophagus features a rectangular base formed by a floor slab, two side slabs, and two end slabs. A rounded, domed lid rests on top. This sarcophagus type is characteristic of royal tombs in the Kinki region during the 5th century. In Gunma Prefecture, only the [[Ota Tenjinyama Kofun]] shares this feature. Few examples are found elsewhere in the Kanto regionOther examples include Takayanagi Chōshizuka Kofun and San-no-Funeme Ōtsukayama Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.. The craftsmanship and materials suggest that artisans from the Kinki region constructed the sarcophagus locally, underscoring strong ties with the Kinki region. Historical records such as the Edo-period "Hatsuhunreki" and "Isesaki Fudoki" also document the sarcophagus. A replica is displayed at the Gunma Prefectural Museum of History. === Other Artifacts === Artifacts include imitation stone knives made from steatite. File:お富士山古墳 出土品.JPG|Artifacts

The mound, built during the mid-5th century, is notable for its use of a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a characteristic feature of royal tombs in the Kinki region. == Overview == Situated in the northwestern part of Isesaki City, Ofujiyama Kofun is the largest keyhole-shaped kofun in the city. The anterior section faces west-southwest, but part of it was removed to accommodate the construction of the [[Ryōmō Line]]. A Fujisan Shrine (dedicated to the deity of Mount Fuji) sits atop the posterior section, from which the kofun derives its name. Artifacts suggest the mound was constructed in the mid-5th century. Its burial facility features a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a hallmark of royal tombs from the Kinki region during the same period. The site was designated a city historic site in 1966 and the sarcophagus was classified as a Prefectural Important Cultural Property in 2001. == Structure == The dimensions of the kofun are as followsDimensions based on the explanation board at the site.: * Total length: 125 meters * '''Anterior section''' ''' Width: 83.2 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters ''' Some sections removed for the Ryōmō Line construction. * '''Posterior section''' ''' Diameter: 77.2 meters ''' Height: 9.5 meters The mound's orientation is aqqroximately 74° east of north. It features a three-tiered structure covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and lined with large cylindrical haniwa. Surrounding the mound is a shield-shaped moat, which extends the total length of the site to 192 meters. == Artifacts == === Nagamochi-style Sarcophagus === [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun sekkan.JPG|thumb|240px|right|{{center|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important Cultural Property)}}]] [[File:お富士山古墳石棺（復元）.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Replica of the sarcophagus}}{{small|Displayed at the National Museum of Japanese History.}}]] The [[Nagamochi-style sarcophagus]] from Ofujiyama Kofun is preserved near the shrine at the posterior section. It is composed of six sandstone slabs: * Total length: 285 cm * Width: 121 cm * Weight: 6.8 tons The sarcophagus features a rectangular base formed by a floor slab, two side slabs, and two end slabs. A rounded, domed lid rests on top. This sarcophagus type is characteristic of royal tombs in the Kinki region during the 5th century. In Gunma Prefecture, only the [[Ota Tenjinyama Kofun]] shares this feature. Few examples are found elsewhere in the Kanto regionOther examples include Takayanagi Chōshizuka Kofun and San-no-Funeme Ōtsukayama Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.. The craftsmanship and materials suggest that artisans from the Kinki region constructed the sarcophagus locally, underscoring strong ties with the Kinki region. Historical records such as the Edo-period "Hatsuhunreki" and "Isesaki Fudoki" also document the sarcophagus. A replica is displayed at the Gunma Prefectural Museum of History. === Other Artifacts === Artifacts include imitation stone knives made from steatite. File:お富士山古墳 出土品.JPG|Artifacts

{{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] |name=Ofujiyama Kofun |location=[[Isesaki, Gunma|Isesaki]], [[Gunma Prefecture]] |coordinates={{coord|36|20|16.93|N|139|10|19.60|E|region:JP-10_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ofujiyama Kofun}} |type=[[Keyhole-shaped Kofun|Keyhole-shaped]] |construction_period=Mid-5th century |burial=Nagamochi-style sarcophagus |length=125 meters |heritage=Isesaki City Designated Historic Site |cultural_property=Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important Cultural Property) |artifacts=[[Cylindrical haniwa|Cylindrical haniwa]] |image=[[File:Ofujiyama-kofun zenkei-2.JPG|300px]]
Panoramic view
{{small|Right: anterior section; left: posterior section. Foreground: Ryōmō Line tracks.}} }} [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun zenkei-1.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Panoramic view from the bridge}}{{small|Left: anterior section; right: posterior section.}}]] [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun funcho.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Posterior section}}{{small|Fujisan Shrine and sarcophagus (far right).}}]] '''Ofujiyama Kofun''' (お富士山古墳), also referred to as御富士山古墳, is a keyhole-shaped Kofun located in [[Isesaki, Gunma|Isesaki City]], [[Gunma Prefecture]]. It has been designated as a historic site by Isesaki City, and its Nagamochi-style sarcophagus is recognized as a Prefectural Important Cultural Property. The mound, built during the mid-5th century, is notable for its use of a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a characteristic feature of royal tombs in the Kinki region. == Overview == Situated in the northwestern part of Isesaki City, Ofujiyama Kofun is the largest keyhole-shaped kofun in the city. The anterior section faces west-southwest, but part of it was removed to accommodate the construction of the [[Ryōmō Line]]. A Fujisan Shrine (dedicated to the deity of Mount Fuji) sits atop the posterior section, from which the kofun derives its name. Artifacts suggest the mound was constructed in the mid-5th century. Its burial facility features a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a hallmark of royal tombs from the Kinki region during the same period. The site was designated a city historic site in 1966 and the sarcophagus was classified as a Prefectural Important Cultural Property in 2001. == Structure == The dimensions of the kofun are as followsDimensions based on the explanation board at the site.: * Total length: 125 meters * '''Anterior section''' ''' Width: 83.2 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters ''' Some sections removed for the Ryōmō Line construction. * '''Posterior section''' ''' Diameter: 77.2 meters ''' Height: 9.5 meters The mound's orientation is [[Category:pp error]]approximately 74° east of north. It features a three-tiered structure covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and lined with large cylindrical haniwa. Surrounding the mound is a shield-shaped moat, which extends the total length of the site to 192 meters. == Artifacts == === Nagamochi-style Sarcophagus === [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun sekkan.JPG|thumb|240px|right|{{center|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important Cultural Property)}}]] [[File:お富士山古墳石棺（復元）.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Replica of the sarcophagus}}{{small|Displayed at the National Museum of Japanese History.}}]] The [[Nagamochi-style sarcophagus]] from Ofujiyama Kofun is preserved near the shrine at the posterior section. It is composed of six sandstone slabs: * Total length: 285 cm * Width: 121 cm * Weight: 6.8 tons The sarcophagus features a rectangular base formed by a floor slab, two side slabs, and two end slabs. A rounded, domed lid rests on top. This sarcophagus type is characteristic of royal tombs in the Kinki region during the 5th century. In Gunma Prefecture, only the [[Ota Tenjinyama Kofun]] shares this feature. Few examples are found elsewhere in the Kanto regionOther examples include Takayanagi Chōshizuka Kofun and San-no-Funeme Ōtsukayama Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.. The craftsmanship and materials suggest that artisans from the Kinki region constructed the sarcophagus locally, underscoring strong ties with the Kinki region. Historical records such as the Edo-period "Hatsuhunreki" and "Isesaki Fudoki" also document the sarcophagus. A replica is displayed at the Gunma Prefectural Museum of History. === Other Artifacts === Artifacts include imitation stone knives made from steatite. File:お富士山古墳 出土品.JPG|Artifacts
{{small|Exhibited at the Isesaki City Akabori History and Folklore Museum.}} == Cultural Properties == === Gunma Prefecture === * '''Important Cultural Property (Tangible Cultural Property)''' ''' Nagamochi-style sarcophagus from Ofujiyama Kofun (archaeological material) – Designated March 23, 2001. === Isesaki City === * '''Historic Site''' ''' Ofujiyama Kofun – Designated April 12, 1966. == In Gunma Prefecture, only the [[Ota Tenjinyama Kofun]] shares this feature. Few examples are found elsewhere in the Kanto regionOther examples include Takayanagi Chōshizuka Kofun and San-no-Funeme Ōtsukayama Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.. The craftsmanship and materials suggest that artisans from the Kinki region constructed the sarcophagus locally, underscoring strong ties with the Kinki region. Historical records such as the Edo-period "Hatsuhunreki" and "Isesaki Fudoki" also document the sarcophagus. A replica is displayed at the Gunma Prefectural Museum of History. === Other Artifacts === Artifacts include imitation stone knives made from steatite. File:お富士山古墳 出土品.JPG|Artifacts

{{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] |name=Ofujiyama Kofun |location=[[Isesaki, Gunma|Isesaki]], [[Gunma Prefecture]] |coordinates={{coord|36|20|16.93|N|139|10|19.60|E|region:JP-10_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ofujiyama Kofun}} |type=[[Keyhole-shaped Kofun|Keyhole-shaped]] |construction_period=Mid-5th century |burial=Nagamochi-style sarcophagus |length=125 meters |heritage=Isesaki City Designated Historic Site |cultural_property=Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important Cultural Property) |artifacts=[[Cylindrical haniwa|Cylindrical haniwa]] |image=[[File:Ofujiyama-kofun zenkei-2.JPG|300px]]
Panoramic view
{{small|Right: anterior section; left: posterior section. Foreground: Ryōmō Line tracks.}} }} [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun zenkei-1.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Panoramic view from the bridge}}{{small|Left: anterior section; right: posterior section.}}]] [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun funcho.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Posterior section}}{{small|Fujisan Shrine and sarcophagus (far right).}}]] '''Ofujiyama Kofun''' (お富士山古墳), also referred to as御富士山古墳, is a keyhole-shaped Kofun located in [[Isesaki, Gunma|Isesaki City]], [[Gunma Prefecture]]. It has been designated as a historic site by Isesaki City, and its Nagamochi-style sarcophagus is recognized as a Prefectural Important Cultural Property. The mound, built during the mid-5th century, is notable for its use of a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a characteristic feature of royal tombs in the Kinki region. == Overview == Situated in the northwestern part of Isesaki City, Ofujiyama Kofun is the largest keyhole-shaped kofun in the city. The anterior section faces west-southwest, but part of it was removed to accommodate the construction of the [[Ryōmō Line]]. A Fujisan Shrine (dedicated to the deity of Mount Fuji) sits atop the posterior section, from which the kofun derives its name. Artifacts suggest the mound was constructed in the mid-5th century. Its burial facility features a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a hallmark of royal tombs from the Kinki region during the same period. The site was designated a city historic site in 1966 and the sarcophagus was classified as a Prefectural Important Cultural Property in 2001. == Structure == The dimensions of the kofun are as followsDimensions based on the explanation board at the site.: * Total length: 125 meters * '''Anterior section''' ''' Width: 83.2 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters ''' Some sections removed for the Ryōmō Line construction. * '''Posterior section''' ''' Diameter: 77.2 meters ''' Height: 9.5 meters The mound's orientation is [[Category:pp error]]approximately 74° east of north. It features a three-tiered structure covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and lined with large cylindrical haniwa. Surrounding the mound is a shield-shaped moat, which extends the total length of the site to 192 meters. == Artifacts == === Nagamochi-style Sarcophagus === [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun sekkan.JPG|thumb|240px|right|{{center|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important Cultural Property)}}]] [[File:お富士山古墳石棺（復元）.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Replica of the sarcophagus}}{{small|Displayed at the National Museum of Japanese History.}}]] The [[Nagamochi-style sarcophagus]] from Ofujiyama Kofun is preserved near the shrine at the posterior section. It is composed of six sandstone slabs: * Total length: 285 cm * Width: 121 cm * Weight: 6.8 tons The sarcophagus features a rectangular base formed by a floor slab, two side slabs, and two end slabs. A rounded, domed lid rests on top. This sarcophagus type is characteristic of royal tombs in the Kinki region during the 5th century. In Gunma Prefecture, only the [[Ota Tenjinyama Kofun]] shares this feature. Few examples are found elsewhere in the Kanto regionOther examples include Takayanagi Chōshizuka Kofun and San-no-Funeme Ōtsukayama Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.. The craftsmanship and materials suggest that artisans from the Kinki region constructed the sarcophagus locally, underscoring strong ties with the Kinki region. Historical records such as the Edo-period "Hatsuhunreki" and "Isesaki Fudoki" also document the sarcophagus. A replica is displayed at the Gunma Prefectural Museum of History. === Other Artifacts === Artifacts include imitation stone knives made from steatite. File:お富士山古墳 出土品.JPG|Artifacts

{{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] |name=Ofujiyama Kofun |location=[[Isesaki, Gunma|Isesaki]], [[Gunma Prefecture]] |coordinates={{coord|36|20|16.93|N|139|10|19.60|E|region:JP-10_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ofujiyama Kofun}} |type=[[Keyhole-shaped Kofun|Keyhole-shaped]] |construction_period=Mid-5th century |burial=Nagamochi-style sarcophagus |length=125 meters |heritage=Isesaki City Designated Historic Site |cultural_property=Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important Cultural Property) |artifacts=[[Cylindrical haniwa|Cylindrical haniwa]] |image=[[File:Ofujiyama-kofun zenkei-2.JPG|300px]]
Panoramic view
{{small|Right: anterior section; left: posterior section. Foreground: Ryōmō Line tracks.}} }} [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun zenkei-1.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Panoramic view from the bridge}}{{small|Left: anterior section; right: posterior section.}}]] [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun funcho.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Posterior section}}{{small|Fujisan Shrine and sarcophagus (far right).}}]] '''Ofujiyama Kofun''' (お富士山古墳), also referred to as御富士山古墳, is a keyhole-shaped Kofun located in [[Isesaki, Gunma|Isesaki City]], [[Gunma Prefecture]]. It has been designated as a historic site by Isesaki City, and its Nagamochi-style sarcophagus is recognized as a Prefectural Important Cultural Property. The mound, built during the mid-5th century, is notable for its use of a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a characteristic feature of royal tombs in the Kinki region. == Overview == Situated in the northwestern part of Isesaki City, Ofujiyama Kofun is the largest keyhole-shaped kofun in the city. The anterior section faces west-southwest, but part of it was removed to accommodate the construction of the [[Ryōmō Line]]. A Fujisan Shrine (dedicated to the deity of Mount Fuji) sits atop the posterior section, from which the kofun derives its name. Artifacts suggest the mound was constructed in the mid-5th century. Its burial facility features a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a hallmark of royal tombs from the Kinki region during the same period. The site was designated a city historic site in 1966 and the sarcophagus was classified as a Prefectural Important Cultural Property in 2001. == Structure == The dimensions of the kofun are as followsDimensions based on the explanation board at the site.: * Total length: 125 meters * '''Anterior section''' ''' Width: 83.2 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters ''' Some sections removed for the Ryōmō Line construction. * '''Posterior section''' ''' Diameter: 77.2 meters ''' Height: 9.5 meters The mound's orientation is [[Category:pp error]]approximately 74° east of north. It features a three-tiered structure covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and lined with large cylindrical haniwa. Surrounding the mound is a shield-shaped moat, which extends the total length of the site to 192 meters. == Artifacts == === Nagamochi-style Sarcophagus === [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun sekkan.JPG|thumb|240px|right|{{center|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important Cultural Property)}}]] [[File:お富士山古墳石棺（復元）.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Replica of the sarcophagus}}{{small|Displayed at the National Museum of Japanese History.}}]] The [[Nagamochi-style sarcophagus]] from Ofujiyama Kofun is preserved near the shrine at the posterior section. It is composed of six sandstone slabs: * Total length: 285 cm * Width: 121 cm * Weight: 6.8 tons The sarcophagus features a rectangular base formed by a floor slab, two side slabs, and two end slabs. A rounded, domed lid rests on top. This sarcophagus type is characteristic of royal tombs in the Kinki region during the 5th century. In Gunma Prefecture, only the [[Ota Tenjinyama Kofun]] shares this feature. Few examples are found elsewhere in the Kanto regionOther examples include Takayanagi Chōshizuka Kofun and San-no-Funeme Ōtsukayama Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.. The craftsmanship and materials suggest that artisans from the Kinki region constructed the sarcophagus locally, underscoring strong ties with the Kinki region. Historical records such as the Edo-period "Hatsuhunreki" and "Isesaki Fudoki" also document the sarcophagus. A replica is displayed at the Gunma Prefectural Museum of History. === Other Artifacts === Artifacts include imitation stone knives made from steatite. File:お富士山古墳 出土品.JPG|Artifacts
{{small|Exhibited at the Isesaki City Akabori History and Folklore Museum.}} == Cultural Properties == === Gunma Prefecture === * '''Important Cultural Property (Tangible Cultural Property)''' ''' This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] |name=Ofujiyama Kofun |location=[[Isesaki, Gunma|Isesaki]], [[Gunma Prefecture]] |coordinates={{coord|36|20|16.93|N|139|10|19.60|E|region:JP-10_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ofujiyama Kofun}} |type=[[Keyhole-shaped Kofun|Keyhole-shaped]] |construction_period=Mid-5th century |burial=Nagamochi-style sarcophagus |length=125 meters |heritage=Isesaki City Designated Historic Site |cultural_property=Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important Cultural Property) |artifacts=[[Cylindrical haniwa|Cylindrical haniwa]] |image=[[File:Ofujiyama-kofun zenkei-2.JPG|300px]]
Panoramic view
{{small|Right: anterior section; left: posterior section. Foreground: Ryōmō Line tracks.}} }} [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun zenkei-1.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Panoramic view from the bridge}}{{small|Left: anterior section; right: posterior section.}}]] [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun funcho.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Posterior section}}{{small|Fujisan Shrine and sarcophagus (far right).}}]] '''Ofujiyama Kofun''' (お富士山古墳), also referred to as御富士山古墳, is a keyhole-shaped Kofun located in [[Isesaki, Gunma|Isesaki City]], [[Gunma Prefecture]]. It has been designated as a historic site by Isesaki City, and its Nagamochi-style sarcophagus is recognized as a Prefectural Important Cultural Property. The mound, built during the mid-5th century, is notable for its use of a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a characteristic feature of royal tombs in the Kinki region. == Overview == Situated in the northwestern part of Isesaki City, Ofujiyama Kofun is the largest keyhole-shaped kofun in the city. The anterior section faces west-southwest, but part of it was removed to accommodate the construction of the [[Ryōmō Line]]. A Fujisan Shrine (dedicated to the deity of Mount Fuji) sits atop the posterior section, from which the kofun derives its name. Artifacts suggest the mound was constructed in the mid-5th century. Its burial facility features a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a hallmark of royal tombs from the Kinki region during the same period. The site was designated a city historic site in 1966 and the sarcophagus was classified as a Prefectural Important Cultural Property in 2001. == Structure == The dimensions of the kofun are as followsDimensions based on the explanation board at the site.: * Total length: 125 meters * '''Anterior section''' ''' Width: 83.2 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters ''' Some sections removed for the Ryōmō Line construction. * '''Posterior section''' ''' Diameter: 77.2 meters ''' Height: 9.5 meters The mound's orientation is [[Category:pp error]]approximately 74° east of north. It features a three-tiered structure covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and lined with large cylindrical haniwa. Surrounding the mound is a shield-shaped moat, which extends the total length of the site to 192 meters. == Artifacts == === Nagamochi-style Sarcophagus === [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun sekkan.JPG|thumb|240px|right|{{center|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important Cultural Property)}}]] [[File:お富士山古墳石棺（復元）.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Replica of the sarcophagus}}{{small|Displayed at the National Museum of Japanese History.}}]] The [[Nagamochi-style sarcophagus]] from Ofujiyama Kofun is preserved near the shrine at the posterior section. It is composed of six sandstone slabs: * Total length: 285 cm * Width: 121 cm * Weight: 6.8 tons The sarcophagus features a rectangular base formed by a floor slab, two side slabs, and two end slabs. A rounded, domed lid rests on top. This sarcophagus type is characteristic of royal tombs in the Kinki region during the 5th century. In Gunma Prefecture, only the [[Ota Tenjinyama Kofun]] shares this feature. Few examples are found elsewhere in the Kanto regionOther examples include Takayanagi Chōshizuka Kofun and San-no-Funeme Ōtsukayama Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.. The craftsmanship and materials suggest that artisans from the Kinki region constructed the sarcophagus locally, underscoring strong ties with the Kinki region. Historical records such as the Edo-period "Hatsuhunreki" and "Isesaki Fudoki" also document the sarcophagus. A replica is displayed at the Gunma Prefectural Museum of History. === Other Artifacts === Artifacts include imitation stone knives made from steatite. File:お富士山古墳 出土品.JPG|Artifacts
{{small|Exhibited at the Isesaki City Akabori History and Folklore Museum.}} == Cultural Properties == === Gunma Prefecture === * '''Important Cultural Property (Tangible Cultural Property)''' ''' Nagamochi-style sarcophagus from Ofujiyama Kofun (archaeological material) – Designated March 23, 2001. === Isesaki City === * '''Historic Site''' ''' Ofujiyama Kofun – Designated April 12, 1966. ==
