{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q17228034]] Wikidata name: Ofujiyama-kofun [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=See as this is a member[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 22:44, 22 April 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=trimming these off. the parameter works as "burial" so no need to use "burial"[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:35, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=added parameters Interred_individual and Mausoleum is planned to be added but I messed up in template editing[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:40, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=now we have a 'burial' and 'burial goods' parameter. So a lot of these pages might not be marked on our wiki as having unknown parameters. But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:02, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] |name=Ofujiyama Kofun |location=[[:en:Isesaki, Gunma|Isesaki]], [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]] |coordinates={{Coord|36.338036|N|139.172111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |type=[[Keyhole-shaped Kofun|Keyhole-shaped]] |built=Mid-5th century |burial=Nagamochi-style sarcophagus |length=125 meters |heritage=Isesaki City Designated Historic Site |notes=Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important designation1) |burial goods=[[Cylindrical Haniwa|Cylindrical haniwa]] |image=File:Ofujiyama-kofun zenkei-1.JPG |architectural_styles={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|qid=Q11268718}} }} [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun zenkei-1.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Panoramic view from the bridge}}{{small|Left: anterior section; right: posterior section.}}]] [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun funcho.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Posterior section}}{{small|Fujisan Shrine and sarcophagus (far right).}}]] '''Ofujiyama Kofun''' (お富士山古墳), also referred to as御富士山古墳, is a keyhole-shaped Kofun located in [[:en:Isesaki, Gunma|Isesaki City]], [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]]. It has been Historic Site1 as a historic site by Isesaki City, and its Nagamochi-style sarcophagus is recognized as a Prefectural Important designation1. The mound, built during the mid-5th century, is notable for its use of a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a characteristic feature of royal tombs in the Kinki region. == Overview == Situated in the northwestern part of Isesaki City, Ofujiyama Kofun is the largest keyhole-shaped kofun in the city. The anterior section faces west-southwest, but part of it was removed to accommodate the construction of the [[:en:Ryōmō Line|Ryōmō Line]]. A Fujisan Shrine (dedicated to the deity of Mount Fuji) sits atop the posterior section, from which the kofun derives its name. burial goods suggest the mound was constructed in the mid-5th century. Its burial facility features a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a hallmark of royal tombs from the Kinki region during the same period. The site was Historic Site1 a city historic site in 1966 and the sarcophagus was classified as a Prefectural Important designation1 in 2001. == burial == The area of the kofun are as followsarea based on the explanation board at the site.: * Total length: 125 meters * '''Anterior section''' ''' Width: 83.2 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters ''' Some sections removed for the Ryōmō Line construction. * '''Posterior section''' ''' Diameter: 77.2 meters ''' Height: 9.5 meters The mound's orientation is approximately 74° east of north. It features a three-tiered structure covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and lined with large cylindrical haniwa. Surrounding the mound is a shield-shaped moat, which extends the total length of the site to 192 meters. == burial goods == === Nagamochi-style Sarcophagus === [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun sekkan.JPG|thumb|240px|right|{{center|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important designation1)}}]] [[File:お富士山古墳石棺（復元）.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Replica of the sarcophagus}}{{small|Displayed at the National Museum of Japanese History.}}]] The [[Chest-shaped stone coffin|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus]] from Ofujiyama Kofun is preserved near the shrine at the posterior section. It is composed of six sandstone slabs: * Total length: 285 cm * Width: 121 cm * Weight: 6.8 tons The sarcophagus features a rectangular base typeed by a floor slab, two side slabs, and two end slabs. A rounded, domed lid rests on top. This sarcophagus type is characteristic of royal tombs in the Kinki region during the 5th century. In Gunma Prefecture, only the [[Ōta Tenjinyama Kofun|Ota Tenjinyama Kofun]] shares this feature. Few examples are found elsewhere in the Kanto regionOther examples include Takayanagi Chōshizuka Kofun and San-no-Funeme Ōtsukayama Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.. The craftsmanship and materials suggest that artisans from the Kinki region constructed the sarcophagus locally, underscoring strong ties with the Kinki region. Historical records such as the Edo-period "Hatsuhunreki" and "Isesaki Fudoki" also document the sarcophagus. A replica is displayed at the Gunma Prefectural Museum of History. === Other burial goods === burial goods include imitation stone knives made from steatite. File:お富士山古墳 Excavated Artifacts.JPG|burial goods

This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] |name=Ofujiyama Kofun |location=[[:en:Isesaki, Gunma|Isesaki]], [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]] |coordinates={{Coord|36.338036|N|139.172111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |type=[[Keyhole-shaped Kofun|Keyhole-shaped]] |built=Mid-5th century |burial=Nagamochi-style sarcophagus |length=125 meters |heritage=Isesaki City Designated Historic Site |notes=Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important designation1) |burial goods=[[Cylindrical Haniwa|Cylindrical haniwa]] |image=File:Ofujiyama-kofun zenkei-1.JPG |architectural_styles={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|qid=Q11268718|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} }} [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun zenkei-1.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Panoramic view from the bridge}}{{small|Left: anterior section; right: posterior section.}}]] [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun funcho.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Posterior section}}{{small|Fujisan Shrine and sarcophagus (far right).}}]] '''Ofujiyama Kofun''' (お富士山古墳), also referred to as御富士山古墳, is a keyhole-shaped Kofun located in [[:en:Isesaki, Gunma|Isesaki City]], [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]]. It has been Historic Site1 as a historic site by Isesaki City, and its Nagamochi-style sarcophagus is recognized as a Prefectural Important designation1. The mound, built during the mid-5th century, is notable for its use of a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a characteristic feature of royal tombs in the Kinki region. == Overview == Situated in the northwestern part of Isesaki City, Ofujiyama Kofun is the largest keyhole-shaped kofun in the city. The anterior section faces west-southwest, but part of it was removed to accommodate the construction of the [[:en:Ryōmō Line|Ryōmō Line]]. A Fujisan Shrine (dedicated to the deity of Mount Fuji) sits atop the posterior section, from which the kofun derives its name. burial goods suggest the mound was constructed in the mid-5th century. Its burial facility features a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a hallmark of royal tombs from the Kinki region during the same period. The site was Historic Site1 a city historic site in 1966 and the sarcophagus was classified as a Prefectural Important designation1 in 2001. == burial == The area of the kofun are as followsarea based on the explanation board at the site.: * Total length: 125 meters * '''Anterior section''' ''' Width: 83.2 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters ''' Some sections removed for the Ryōmō Line construction. * '''Posterior section''' ''' Diameter: 77.2 meters ''' Height: 9.5 meters The mound's orientation is approximately 74° east of north. It features a three-tiered structure covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and lined with large cylindrical haniwa. Surrounding the mound is a shield-shaped moat, which extends the total length of the site to 192 meters. == burial goods == === Nagamochi-style Sarcophagus === [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun sekkan.JPG|thumb|240px|right|{{center|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important designation1)}}]] [[File:お富士山古墳石棺（復元）.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Replica of the sarcophagus}}{{small|Displayed at the National Museum of Japanese History.}}]] The [[Chest-shaped stone coffin|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus]] from Ofujiyama Kofun is preserved near the shrine at the posterior section. It is composed of six sandstone slabs: * Total length: 285 cm * Width: 121 cm * Weight: 6.8 tons The sarcophagus features a rectangular base typeed by a floor slab, two side slabs, and two end slabs. A rounded, domed lid rests on top. This sarcophagus type is characteristic of royal tombs in the Kinki region during the 5th century. In Gunma Prefecture, only the [[Ōta Tenjinyama Kofun|Ota Tenjinyama Kofun]] shares this feature. Few examples are found elsewhere in the Kanto regionOther examples include Takayanagi Chōshizuka Kofun and San-no-Funeme Ōtsukayama Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.. The craftsmanship and materials suggest that artisans from the Kinki region constructed the sarcophagus locally, underscoring strong ties with the Kinki region. Historical records such as the Edo-period "Hatsuhunreki" and "Isesaki Fudoki" also document the sarcophagus. A replica is displayed at the Gunma Prefectural Museum of History. === Other burial goods === burial goods include imitation stone knives made from steatite. File:お富士山古墳 Excavated Artifacts.JPG|burial goods
{{small|Exhibited at the Isesaki City Akabori History and Folklore Museum.}} == Historic Site == === Gunma Prefecture === * '''Important designation1 (Tangible designation1)''' ''' Nagamochi-style sarcophagus from Ofujiyama Kofun (archaeological material) – Designated March 23, 2001. === Isesaki City === * '''Historic Site''' ''' Ofujiyama Kofun – Designated April 12, 1966. == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Explanation Board at the Site (Isesaki City Board of Education, installed February 5, 1999) * Sarcophagus Preservation Facility Documentation == Related Sites == * [[:en:Keno Province|Keno]] * [[Ōta Tenjinyama Kofun|Ota Tenjinyama Kofun]] – The largest kofun in eastern Japan, also featuring a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Ofujiyama Kofun}} * [https://www.city.isesaki.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/shitei/shiseki/3380.html Ofujiyama Kofun] (Isesaki City Website) * [http://www.gunmaibun.org/remain/guide/tyumo/ohujiyama.html Ofujiyama Kofun] (Gunma Archaeological Research Center) {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|お富士山古墳|version=101597472|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] [[Category:Isesaki, Gunma]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] }} [[sn:Ofujiyama Kofun]] [[ja:お富士山古墳]] [[Category:History of Isesaki, Gunma]] [[Category:Kofun of Gunma prefecture]] [[Category:Municipal Historic Sites in Gunma Prefecture]] {{Commons category|Ofujiyama Kofun}} {{wikidata link|Q17228034}}

The site was Historic Site1 a city historic site in 1966 and the sarcophagus was classified as a Prefectural Important designation1 in 2001. == burial == The area of the kofun are as followsarea based on the explanation board at the site.: * Total length: 125 meters * '''Anterior section''' ''' Width: 83.2 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters ''' Some sections removed for the Ryōmō Line construction. * '''Posterior section''' ''' Diameter: 77.2 meters ''' Height: 9.5 meters The mound's orientation is approximately 74° east of north. It features a three-tiered structure covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and lined with large cylindrical haniwa. Surrounding the mound is a shield-shaped moat, which extends the total length of the site to 192 meters. == burial goods == === Nagamochi-style Sarcophagus === [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun sekkan.JPG|thumb|240px|right|{{center|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important designation1)}}]] [[File:お富士山古墳石棺（復元）.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Replica of the sarcophagus}}{{small|Displayed at the National Museum of Japanese History.}}]] The [[Chest-shaped Sarcophagus|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus]] from Ofujiyama Kofun is preserved near the shrine at the posterior section. It is composed of six sandstone slabs: * Total length: 285 cm * Width: 121 cm * Weight: 6.8 tons The sarcophagus features a rectangular base typeed by a floor slab, two side slabs, and two end slabs. A rounded, domed lid rests on top. This sarcophagus type is characteristic of royal tombs in the Kinki region during the 5th century. In Gunma Prefecture, only the [[Ōta Tenjinyama Kofun|Ota Tenjinyama Kofun]] shares this feature. Few examples are found elsewhere in the Kanto regionOther examples include Takayanagi Chōshizuka Kofun and San-no-Funeme Ōtsukayama Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.. The craftsmanship and materials suggest that artisans from the Kinki region constructed the sarcophagus locally, underscoring strong ties with the Kinki region. Historical records such as the Edo-period "Hatsuhunreki" and "Isesaki Fudoki" also document the sarcophagus. A replica is displayed at the Gunma Prefectural Museum of History. === Other burial goods === burial goods include imitation stone knives made from steatite. [[sn:commons:Category:Ofujiyama Kofun]][[ja:お富士山古墳]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q17228034]] Wikidata name: Ofujiyama-kofun [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=See as this is a member[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 22:44, 22 April 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=trimming these off. the parameter works as "burial" so no need to use "burial"[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:35, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=added parameters Interred_individual and Mausoleum is planned to be added but I messed up in template editing[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:40, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=now we have a 'burial' and 'burial goods' parameter. So a lot of these pages might not be marked on our wiki as having unknown parameters. But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:02, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] |name=Ofujiyama Kofun |location=[[:en:Isesaki, Gunma|Isesaki]], [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]] |coordinates={{Coord|36.338036|N|139.172111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |type=[[Keyhole-shaped Kofun|Keyhole-shaped]] |built=Mid-5th century |burial=Nagamochi-style sarcophagus |length=125 meters |heritage=Isesaki City Designated Historic Site |notes=Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important designation1) |burial goods=[[Cylindrical Haniwa|Cylindrical haniwa]] |image=File:Ofujiyama-kofun zenkei-1.JPG |architectural_styles={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} }} [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun zenkei-1.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Panoramic view from the bridge}}{{small|Left: anterior section; right: posterior section.}}]] [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun funcho.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Posterior section}}{{small|Fujisan Shrine and sarcophagus (far right).}}]] '''Ofujiyama Kofun''' (お富士山古墳), also referred to as御富士山古墳, is a keyhole-shaped Kofun located in [[:en:Isesaki, Gunma|Isesaki City]], [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]]. It has been Historic Site1 as a historic site by Isesaki City, and its Nagamochi-style sarcophagus is recognized as a Prefectural Important designation1. The mound, built during the mid-5th century, is notable for its use of a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a characteristic feature of royal tombs in the Kinki region. == Overview == Situated in the northwestern part of Isesaki City, Ofujiyama Kofun is the largest keyhole-shaped kofun in the city. The anterior section faces west-southwest, but part of it was removed to accommodate the construction of the [[:en:Ryōmō Line|Ryōmō Line]]. A Fujisan Shrine (dedicated to the deity of Mount Fuji) sits atop the posterior section, from which the kofun derives its name. burial goods suggest the mound was constructed in the mid-5th century. Its burial facility features a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a hallmark of royal tombs from the Kinki region during the same period. The site was Historic Site1 a city historic site in 1966 and the sarcophagus was classified as a Prefectural Important designation1 in 2001. == burial == The area of the kofun are as followsarea based on the explanation board at the site.: * Total length: 125 meters * '''Anterior section''' ''' Width: 83.2 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters ''' Some sections removed for the Ryōmō Line construction. * '''Posterior section''' ''' Diameter: 77.2 meters ''' Height: 9.5 meters The mound's orientation is approximately 74° east of north. It features a three-tiered structure covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and lined with large cylindrical haniwa. Surrounding the mound is a shield-shaped moat, which extends the total length of the site to 192 meters. == burial goods == === Nagamochi-style Sarcophagus === [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun sekkan.JPG|thumb|240px|right|{{center|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important designation1)}}]] [[File:お富士山古墳石棺（復元）.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Replica of the sarcophagus}}{{small|Displayed at the National Museum of Japanese History.}}]] The [[Chest-shaped Sarcophagus|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus]] from Ofujiyama Kofun is preserved near the shrine at the posterior section. It is composed of six sandstone slabs: * Total length: 285 cm * Width: 121 cm * Weight: 6.8 tons The sarcophagus features a rectangular base typeed by a floor slab, two side slabs, and two end slabs. A rounded, domed lid rests on top. This sarcophagus type is characteristic of royal tombs in the Kinki region during the 5th century. In Gunma Prefecture, only the [[Ōta Tenjinyama Kofun|Ota Tenjinyama Kofun]] shares this feature. Few examples are found elsewhere in the Kanto regionOther examples include Takayanagi Chōshizuka Kofun and San-no-Funeme Ōtsukayama Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.. The craftsmanship and materials suggest that artisans from the Kinki region constructed the sarcophagus locally, underscoring strong ties with the Kinki region. Historical records such as the Edo-period "Hatsuhunreki" and "Isesaki Fudoki" also document the sarcophagus. A replica is displayed at the Gunma Prefectural Museum of History. === Other burial goods === burial goods include imitation stone knives made from steatite. File:お富士山古墳 Excavated Artifacts.JPG|burial goods
{{small|Exhibited at the Isesaki City Akabori History and Folklore Museum.}} == Historic Site == === Gunma Prefecture === * '''Important designation1 (Tangible designation1)''' ''' Nagamochi-style sarcophagus from Ofujiyama Kofun (archaeological material) – Designated March 23, 2001. === Isesaki City === * '''Historic Site''' ''' Ofujiyama Kofun – Designated April 12, 1966. == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Explanation Board at the Site (Isesaki City Board of Education, installed February 5, 1999) * Sarcophagus Preservation Facility Documentation == Related Sites == * [[:en:Keno Province|Keno]] * [[Ōta Tenjinyama Kofun|Ota Tenjinyama Kofun]] – The largest kofun in eastern Japan, also featuring a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Ofujiyama Kofun}} * [https://www.city.isesaki.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/shitei/shiseki/3380.html Ofujiyama Kofun] (Isesaki City Website) * [http://www.gunmaibun.org/remain/guide/tyumo/ohujiyama.html Ofujiyama Kofun] (Gunma Archaeological Research Center) {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|お富士山古墳|version=101597472|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] [[Category:Isesaki, Gunma]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] }} [[ja:お富士山古墳]] [[Category:History of Isesaki, Gunma]] [[Category:Kofun of Gunma prefecture]] [[Category:Municipal Historic Sites in Gunma Prefecture]] {{Commons category|Ofujiyama Kofun}} {{wikidata link|Q17228034}}

This was added through to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] |name=Ofujiyama Kofun |location=[[:en:Isesaki, Gunma|Isesaki]], [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]] |coordinates={{Coord|36.338036|N|139.172111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |type=[[Keyhole-shaped Kofun|Keyhole-shaped]] |built=Mid-5th century |burial=Nagamochi-style sarcophagus |length=125 meters |heritage=Isesaki City Designated Historic Site |notes=Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important designation1) |burial goods=[[Cylindrical Haniwa|Cylindrical haniwa]] |image=File:Ofujiyama-kofun zenkei-1.JPG |architectural_styles={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} }} [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun zenkei-1.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Panoramic view from the bridge}}{{small|Left: anterior section; right: posterior section.}}]] [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun funcho.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Posterior section}}{{small|Fujisan Shrine and sarcophagus (far right).}}]] '''Ofujiyama Kofun''' (お富士山古墳), also referred to as御富士山古墳, is a keyhole-shaped Kofun located in [[:en:Isesaki, Gunma|Isesaki City]], [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]]. It has been Historic Site1 as a historic site by Isesaki City, and its Nagamochi-style sarcophagus is recognized as a Prefectural Important designation1. The mound, built during the mid-5th century, is notable for its use of a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a characteristic feature of royal tombs in the Kinki region. == Overview == Situated in the northwestern part of Isesaki City, Ofujiyama Kofun is the largest keyhole-shaped kofun in the city. The anterior section faces west-southwest, but part of it was removed to accommodate the construction of the [[:en:Ryōmō Line|Ryōmō Line]]. A Fujisan Shrine (dedicated to the deity of Mount Fuji) sits atop the posterior section, from which the kofun derives its name. burial goods suggest the mound was constructed in the mid-5th century. Its burial facility features a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a hallmark of royal tombs from the Kinki region during the same period. The site was Historic Site1 a city historic site in 1966 and the sarcophagus was classified as a Prefectural Important designation1 in 2001. == burial == The area of the kofun are as followsarea based on the explanation board at the site.: * Total length: 125 meters * '''Anterior section''' ''' Width: 83.2 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters ''' Some sections removed for the Ryōmō Line construction. * '''Posterior section''' ''' Diameter: 77.2 meters ''' Height: 9.5 meters The mound's orientation is approximately 74° east of north. It features a three-tiered structure covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and lined with large cylindrical haniwa. Surrounding the mound is a shield-shaped moat, which extends the total length of the site to 192 meters. == burial goods == === Nagamochi-style Sarcophagus === [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun sekkan.JPG|thumb|240px|right|{{center|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important designation1)}}]] [[File:お富士山古墳石棺（復元）.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Replica of the sarcophagus}}{{small|Displayed at the National Museum of Japanese History.}}]] The [[Chest-shaped Sarcophagus|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus]] from Ofujiyama Kofun is preserved near the shrine at the posterior section. It is composed of six sandstone slabs: * Total length: 285 cm * Width: 121 cm * Weight: 6.8 tons The sarcophagus features a rectangular base typeed by a floor slab, two side slabs, and two end slabs. A rounded, domed lid rests on top. This sarcophagus type is characteristic of royal tombs in the Kinki region during the 5th century. In Gunma Prefecture, only the [[Ōta Tenjinyama Kofun|Ota Tenjinyama Kofun]] shares this feature. Few examples are found elsewhere in the Kanto regionOther examples include Takayanagi Chōshizuka Kofun and San-no-Funeme Ōtsukayama Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.. The craftsmanship and materials suggest that artisans from the Kinki region constructed the sarcophagus locally, underscoring strong ties with the Kinki region. Historical records such as the Edo-period "Hatsuhunreki" and "Isesaki Fudoki" also document the sarcophagus. A replica is displayed at the Gunma Prefectural Museum of History. === Other burial goods === burial goods include imitation stone knives made from steatite. File:お富士山古墳 Excavated Artifacts.JPG|burial goods
{{small|Exhibited at the Isesaki City Akabori History and Folklore Museum.}} == Historic Site == === Gunma Prefecture === * '''Important designation1 (Tangible designation1)''' ''' Nagamochi-style sarcophagus from Ofujiyama Kofun (archaeological material) – Designated March 23, 2001. === Isesaki City === * '''Historic Site''' ''' Ofujiyama Kofun – Designated April 12, 1966. == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Explanation Board at the Site (Isesaki City Board of Education, installed February 5, 1999) * Sarcophagus Preservation Facility Documentation == Related Sites == * [[:en:Keno Province|Keno]] * [[Ōta Tenjinyama Kofun|Ota Tenjinyama Kofun]] – The largest kofun in eastern Japan, also featuring a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Ofujiyama Kofun}} * [https://www.city.isesaki.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/shitei/shiseki/3380.html Ofujiyama Kofun] (Isesaki City Website) * [http://www.gunmaibun.org/remain/guide/tyumo/ohujiyama.html Ofujiyama Kofun] (Gunma Archaeological Research Center) {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|お富士山古墳|version=101597472|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] [[Category:Isesaki, Gunma]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] }} [[ja:お富士山古墳]] [[Category:History of Isesaki, Gunma]] [[Category:Kofun of Gunma prefecture]] [[Category:Municipal Historic Sites in Gunma Prefecture]] {{Commons category|Ofujiyama Kofun}} {{wikidata link|Q17228034}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

This was added through to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] |name=Ofujiyama Kofun |location=[[:en:Isesaki, Gunma|Isesaki]], [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]] |coordinates={{Coord|36.338036|N|139.172111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |type=[[Keyhole-shaped Kofun|Keyhole-shaped]] |built=Mid-5th century |burial=Nagamochi-style sarcophagus |length=125 meters |heritage=Isesaki City Designated Historic Site |notes=Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important designation1) |burial goods=[[Cylindrical Haniwa|Cylindrical haniwa]] |image=File:Ofujiyama-kofun zenkei-1.JPG |architectural_styles={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} }} [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun zenkei-1.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Panoramic view from the bridge}}{{small|Left: anterior section; right: posterior section.}}]] [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun funcho.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Posterior section}}{{small|Fujisan Shrine and sarcophagus (far right).}}]] '''Ofujiyama Kofun''' (お富士山古墳), also referred to as御富士山古墳, is a keyhole-shaped Kofun located in [[:en:Isesaki, Gunma|Isesaki City]], [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]]. It has been Historic Site1 as a historic site by Isesaki City, and its Nagamochi-style sarcophagus is recognized as a Prefectural Important designation1. The mound, built during the mid-5th century, is notable for its use of a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a characteristic feature of royal tombs in the Kinki region. == Overview == Situated in the northwestern part of Isesaki City, Ofujiyama Kofun is the largest keyhole-shaped kofun in the city. The anterior section faces west-southwest, but part of it was removed to accommodate the construction of the [[:en:Ryōmō Line|Ryōmō Line]]. A Fujisan Shrine (dedicated to the deity of Mount Fuji) sits atop the posterior section, from which the kofun derives its name. burial goods suggest the mound was constructed in the mid-5th century. Its burial facility features a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a hallmark of royal tombs from the Kinki region during the same period. The site was Historic Site1 a city historic site in 1966 and the sarcophagus was classified as a Prefectural Important designation1 in 2001. == burial == The area of the kofun are as followsarea based on the explanation board at the site.: * Total length: 125 meters * '''Anterior section''' ''' Width: 83.2 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters ''' Some sections removed for the Ryōmō Line construction. * '''Posterior section''' ''' Diameter: 77.2 meters ''' Height: 9.5 meters The mound's orientation is approximately 74° east of north. It features a three-tiered structure covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and lined with large cylindrical haniwa. Surrounding the mound is a shield-shaped moat, which extends the total length of the site to 192 meters. == burial goods == === Nagamochi-style Sarcophagus === [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun sekkan.JPG|thumb|240px|right|{{center|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important designation1)}}]] [[File:お富士山古墳石棺（復元）.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Replica of the sarcophagus}}{{small|Displayed at the National Museum of Japanese History.}}]] The [[Chest-shaped Sarcophagus|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus]] from Ofujiyama Kofun is preserved near the shrine at the posterior section. It is composed of six sandstone slabs: * Total length: 285 cm * Width: 121 cm * Weight: 6.8 tons The sarcophagus features a rectangular base typeed by a floor slab, two side slabs, and two end slabs. A rounded, domed lid rests on top. This sarcophagus type is characteristic of royal tombs in the Kinki region during the 5th century. In Gunma Prefecture, only the [[Ōta Tenjinyama Kofun|Ota Tenjinyama Kofun]] shares this feature. Few examples are found elsewhere in the Kanto regionOther examples include Takayanagi Chōshizuka Kofun and San-no-Funeme Ōtsukayama Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.. The craftsmanship and materials suggest that artisans from the Kinki region constructed the sarcophagus locally, underscoring strong ties with the Kinki region. Historical records such as the Edo-period "Hatsuhunreki" and "Isesaki Fudoki" also document the sarcophagus. A replica is displayed at the Gunma Prefectural Museum of History. === Other burial goods === burial goods include imitation stone knives made from steatite. File:お富士山古墳 Excavated Artifacts.JPG|burial goods
{{small|Exhibited at the Isesaki City Akabori History and Folklore Museum.}} == Historic Site == === Gunma Prefecture === * '''Important designation1 (Tangible designation1)''' ''' Nagamochi-style sarcophagus from Ofujiyama Kofun (archaeological material) – Designated March 23, 2001. === Isesaki City === * '''Historic Site''' ''' Ofujiyama Kofun – Designated April 12, 1966. == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Explanation Board at the Site (Isesaki City Board of Education, installed February 5, 1999) * Sarcophagus Preservation Facility Documentation == Related Sites == * [[:en:Keno Province|Keno]] * [[Ōta Tenjinyama Kofun|Ota Tenjinyama Kofun]] – The largest kofun in eastern Japan, also featuring a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Ofujiyama Kofun}} * [https://www.city.isesaki.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/shitei/shiseki/3380.html Ofujiyama Kofun] (Isesaki City Website) * [http://www.gunmaibun.org/remain/guide/tyumo/ohujiyama.html Ofujiyama Kofun] (Gunma Archaeological Research Center) {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|お富士山古墳|version=101597472|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] [[Category:Isesaki, Gunma]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] }} [[ja:お富士山古墳]] [[Category:History of Isesaki, Gunma]] [[Category:Kofun of Gunma prefecture]] [[Category:Municipal Historic Sites in Gunma Prefecture]] {{Commons category|Ofujiyama Kofun}} {{wikidata link|Q17228034}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

It has been Historic Site1 as a historic site by Isesaki City, and its Nagamochi-style sarcophagus is recognized as a Prefectural Important designation1. The mound, built during the mid-5th century, is notable for its use of a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a characteristic feature of royal tombs in the Kinki region. == Overview == Situated in the northwestern part of Isesaki City, Ofujiyama Kofun is the largest keyhole-shaped kofun in the city. The anterior section faces west-southwest, but part of it was removed to accommodate the construction of the [[:en:Ryōmō Line|Ryōmō Line]]. A Fujisan Shrine (dedicated to the deity of Mount Fuji) sits atop the posterior section, from which the kofun derives its name. burial goods suggest the mound was constructed in the mid-5th century. Its burial facility features a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a hallmark of royal tombs from the Kinki region during the same period. The site was Historic Site1 a city historic site in 1966 and the sarcophagus was classified as a Prefectural Important designation1 in 2001. == burial == The area of the kofun are as followsarea based on the explanation board at the site.: * Total length: 125 meters * '''Anterior section''' ''' Width: 83.2 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters ''' Some sections removed for the Ryōmō Line construction. * '''Posterior section''' ''' Diameter: 77.2 meters ''' Height: 9.5 meters The mound's orientation is approximately 74° east of north. It features a three-tiered structure covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and lined with large cylindrical haniwa. Surrounding the mound is a shield-shaped moat, which extends the total length of the site to 192 meters. == burial goods == === Nagamochi-style Sarcophagus === [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun sekkan.JPG|thumb|240px|right|{{center|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important designation1)}}]] [[File:お富士山古墳石棺（復元）.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Replica of the sarcophagus}}{{small|Displayed at the National Museum of Japanese History.}}]] The [[Chest-shaped Sarcophagus|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus]] from Ofujiyama Kofun is preserved near the shrine at the posterior section. It is composed of six sandstone slabs: * Total length: 285 cm * Width: 121 cm * Weight: 6.8 tons The sarcophagus features a rectangular base typeed by a floor slab, two side slabs, and two end slabs. A rounded, domed lid rests on top. This sarcophagus type is characteristic of royal tombs in the Kinki region during the 5th century. In Gunma Prefecture, only the [[Ōta Tenjinyama Kofun|Ota Tenjinyama Kofun]] shares this feature. Few examples are found elsewhere in the Kanto regionOther examples include Takayanagi Chōshizuka Kofun and San-no-Funeme Ōtsukayama Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.. The craftsmanship and materials suggest that artisans from the Kinki region constructed the sarcophagus locally, underscoring strong ties with the Kinki region. Historical records such as the Edo-period "Hatsuhunreki" and "Isesaki Fudoki" also document the sarcophagus. A replica is displayed at the Gunma Prefectural Museum of History. === Other burial goods === burial goods include imitation stone knives made from steatite. File:お富士山古墳 Excavated Artifacts.JPG|burial goods
{{small|Exhibited at the Isesaki City Akabori History and Folklore Museum.}} == Historic Site == === Gunma Prefecture === * '''Important designation1 (Tangible designation1)''' ''' Nagamochi-style sarcophagus from Ofujiyama Kofun (archaeological material) – Designated March 23, 2001. === Isesaki City === * '''Historic Site''' ''' Ofujiyama Kofun – Designated April 12, 1966. == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Explanation Board at the Site (Isesaki City Board of Education, installed February 5, 1999) * Sarcophagus Preservation Facility Documentation == Related Sites == * [[:en:Keno Province|Keno]] * [[Ōta Tenjinyama Kofun|Ota Tenjinyama Kofun]] – The largest kofun in eastern Japan, also featuring a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Ofujiyama Kofun}} * [https://www.city.isesaki.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/shitei/shiseki/3380.html Ofujiyama Kofun] (Isesaki City Website) * [http://www.gunmaibun.org/remain/guide/tyumo/ohujiyama.html Ofujiyama Kofun] (Gunma Archaeological Research Center) {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|お富士山古墳|version=101597472|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] [[Category:Isesaki, Gunma]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] }} [[ja:お富士山古墳]] [[Category:History of Isesaki, Gunma]] [[Category:Kofun of Gunma prefecture]] [[Category:Municipal Historic Sites in Gunma Prefecture]] {{Commons category|Ofujiyama Kofun}} {{wikidata link|Q17228034}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

The mound, built during the mid-5th century, is notable for its use of a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a characteristic feature of royal tombs in the Kinki region. == Overview == Situated in the northwestern part of Isesaki City, Ofujiyama Kofun is the largest keyhole-shaped kofun in the city. The anterior section faces west-southwest, but part of it was removed to accommodate the construction of the [[:en:Ryōmō Line|Ryōmō Line]]. A Fujisan Shrine (dedicated to the deity of Mount Fuji) sits atop the posterior section, from which the kofun derives its name. burial goods suggest the mound was constructed in the mid-5th century. Its burial facility features a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a hallmark of royal tombs from the Kinki region during the same period. The site was Historic Site1 a city historic site in 1966 and the sarcophagus was classified as a Prefectural Important designation1 in 2001. == burial == The area of the kofun are as followsarea based on the explanation board at the site.: * Total length: 125 meters * '''Anterior section''' ''' Width: 83.2 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters ''' Some sections removed for the Ryōmō Line construction. * '''Posterior section''' ''' Diameter: 77.2 meters ''' Height: 9.5 meters The mound's orientation is approximately 74° east of north. It features a three-tiered structure covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and lined with large cylindrical haniwa. Surrounding the mound is a shield-shaped moat, which extends the total length of the site to 192 meters. == burial goods == === Nagamochi-style Sarcophagus === [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun sekkan.JPG|thumb|240px|right|{{center|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important designation1)}}]] [[File:お富士山古墳石棺（復元）.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Replica of the sarcophagus}}{{small|Displayed at the National Museum of Japanese History.}}]] The [[Chest-shaped Sarcophagus|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus]] from Ofujiyama Kofun is preserved near the shrine at the posterior section. It is composed of six sandstone slabs: * Total length: 285 cm * Width: 121 cm * Weight: 6.8 tons The sarcophagus features a rectangular base typeed by a floor slab, two side slabs, and two end slabs. A rounded, domed lid rests on top. This sarcophagus type is characteristic of royal tombs in the Kinki region during the 5th century. In Gunma Prefecture, only the [[Ōta Tenjinyama Kofun|Ota Tenjinyama Kofun]] shares this feature. Few examples are found elsewhere in the Kanto regionOther examples include Takayanagi Chōshizuka Kofun and San-no-Funeme Ōtsukayama Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.. The craftsmanship and materials suggest that artisans from the Kinki region constructed the sarcophagus locally, underscoring strong ties with the Kinki region. Historical records such as the Edo-period "Hatsuhunreki" and "Isesaki Fudoki" also document the sarcophagus. A replica is displayed at the Gunma Prefectural Museum of History. === Other burial goods === burial goods include imitation stone knives made from steatite. File:お富士山古墳 Excavated Artifacts.JPG|burial goods
{{small|Exhibited at the Isesaki City Akabori History and Folklore Museum.}} == Historic Site == === Gunma Prefecture === * '''Important designation1 (Tangible designation1)''' ''' Nagamochi-style sarcophagus from Ofujiyama Kofun (archaeological material) – Designated March 23, 2001. === Isesaki City === * '''Historic Site''' ''' Ofujiyama Kofun – Designated April 12, 1966. == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Explanation Board at the Site (Isesaki City Board of Education, installed February 5, 1999) * Sarcophagus Preservation Facility Documentation == Related Sites == * [[:en:Keno Province|Keno]] * [[Ōta Tenjinyama Kofun|Ota Tenjinyama Kofun]] – The largest kofun in eastern Japan, also featuring a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Ofujiyama Kofun}} * [https://www.city.isesaki.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/shitei/shiseki/3380.html Ofujiyama Kofun] (Isesaki City Website) * [http://www.gunmaibun.org/remain/guide/tyumo/ohujiyama.html Ofujiyama Kofun] (Gunma Archaeological Research Center) {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|お富士山古墳|version=101597472|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} [[ja:お富士山古墳]] {{Commons category|Ofujiyama Kofun}} {{wikidata link|Q17228034}} [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:History of Isesaki, Gunma]] [[Category:Isesaki, Gunma]] [[Category:Kofun of Gunma prefecture]] [[Category:Municipal Historic Sites in Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]]

A rounded, domed lid rests on top. This sarcophagus type is characteristic of royal tombs in the Kinki region during the 5th century. In Gunma Prefecture, only the [[Ōta Tenjinyama Kofun|Ota Tenjinyama Kofun]] shares this feature. Few examples are found elsewhere in the Kanto regionOther examples include Takayanagi Chōshizuka Kofun and San-no-Funeme Ōtsukayama Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.. The craftsmanship and materials suggest that artisans from the Kinki region constructed the sarcophagus locally, underscoring strong ties with the Kinki region. Historical records such as the Edo-period "Hatsuhunreki" and "Isesaki Fudoki" also document the sarcophagus. A replica is displayed at the Gunma Prefectural Museum of History. === Other burial goods === burial goods include imitation stone knives made from steatite. File:お富士山古墳 Excavated Artifacts.JPG|burial goods

revision.text:
[[co:commons:Category:Ofujiyama Kofun]][[ja:お富士山古墳]] [[co:commons:Category:Ofujiyama Kofun]][[ja:お富士山古墳]] [[sn:commons:Category:Ofujiyama Kofun]][[ja:お富士山古墳]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q17228034]] Wikidata name: Ofujiyama-kofun [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=See as this is a member[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 22:44, 22 April 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=trimming these off. the parameter works as "burial" so no need to use "burial"[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:35, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=added parameters Interred_individual and Mausoleum is planned to be added but I messed up in template editing[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:40, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=now we have a 'burial' and 'burial goods' parameter. So a lot of these pages might not be marked on our wiki as having unknown parameters. But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:02, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] |name=Ofujiyama Kofun |location=[[:en:Isesaki, Gunma|Isesaki]], {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture}} |coordinates={{Coord|36.338036|N|139.172111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |type=[[Keyhole-shaped Kofun|Keyhole-shaped]] |built=Mid-5th century |burial=Nagamochi-style sarcophagus |length=125 meters |heritage=Isesaki City Designated Historic Site |notes=Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important designation1) |burial goods=[[Cylindrical Haniwa|Cylindrical haniwa]] |image=File:Ofujiyama-kofun zenkei-1.JPG |architectural_styles={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} }} [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun zenkei-1.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Panoramic view from the bridge}}{{small|Left: anterior section; right: posterior section.}}]] [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun funcho.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Posterior section}}{{small|Fujisan Shrine and sarcophagus (far right).}}]] '''Ofujiyama Kofun''' (お富士山古墳), also referred to as御富士山古墳, is a keyhole-shaped Kofun located in [[:en:Isesaki, Gunma|Isesaki City]], {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture}}. It has been Historic Site1 as a historic site by Isesaki City, and its Nagamochi-style sarcophagus is recognized as a Prefectural Important designation1. The mound, built during the mid-5th century, is notable for its use of a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a characteristic feature of royal tombs in the Kinki region. == Overview == Situated in the northwestern part of Isesaki City, Ofujiyama Kofun is the largest keyhole-shaped kofun in the city. The anterior section faces west-southwest, but part of it was removed to accommodate the construction of the {{ill|Ryōmō Line|en|Ryōmō Line|WD=Q253391|lt=Ryōmō Line}}. A Fujisan Shrine (dedicated to the deity of Mount Fuji) sits atop the posterior section, from which the kofun derives its name. burial goods suggest the mound was constructed in the mid-5th century. Its burial facility features a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a hallmark of royal tombs from the Kinki region during the same period. The site was Historic Site1 a city historic site in 1966 and the sarcophagus was classified as a Prefectural Important designation1 in 2001. == burial == The area of the kofun are as followsarea based on the explanation board at the site.: * Total length: 125 meters * '''Anterior section''' ''' Width: 83.2 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters ''' Some sections removed for the Ryōmō Line construction. * '''Posterior section''' ''' Diameter: 77.2 meters ''' Height: 9.5 meters The mound's orientation is approximately 74° east of north. It features a three-tiered structure covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and lined with large cylindrical haniwa. Surrounding the mound is a shield-shaped moat, which extends the total length of the site to 192 meters. == burial goods == === Nagamochi-style Sarcophagus === [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun sekkan.JPG|thumb|240px|right|{{center|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important designation1)}}]] [[File:お富士山古墳石棺（復元）.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Replica of the sarcophagus}}{{small|Displayed at the National Museum of Japanese History.}}]] The [[Chest-shaped Sarcophagus|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus]] from Ofujiyama Kofun is preserved near the shrine at the posterior section. It is composed of six sandstone slabs: * Total length: 285 cm * Width: 121 cm * Weight: 6.8 tons The sarcophagus features a rectangular base typeed by a floor slab, two side slabs, and two end slabs. A rounded, domed lid rests on top. This sarcophagus type is characteristic of royal tombs in the Kinki region during the 5th century. In Gunma Prefecture, only the [[Ōta Tenjinyama Kofun|Ota Tenjinyama Kofun]] shares this feature. Few examples are found elsewhere in the Kanto regionOther examples include Takayanagi Chōshizuka Kofun and San-no-Funeme Ōtsukayama Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.. The craftsmanship and materials suggest that artisans from the Kinki region constructed the sarcophagus locally, underscoring strong ties with the Kinki region. Historical records such as the Edo-period "Hatsuhunreki" and "Isesaki Fudoki" also document the sarcophagus. A replica is displayed at the Gunma Prefectural Museum of History. === Other burial goods === burial goods include imitation stone knives made from steatite. File:お富士山古墳 Excavated Artifacts.JPG|burial goods

It has been Historic Site1 as a historic site by Isesaki City, and its Nagamochi-style sarcophagus is recognized as a Prefectural Important designation1. The mound, built during the mid-5th century, is notable for its use of a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a characteristic feature of royal tombs in the Kinki region. == Overview == Situated in the northwestern part of Isesaki City, Ofujiyama Kofun is the largest keyhole-shaped kofun in the city. The anterior section faces west-southwest, but part of it was removed to accommodate the construction of the {{ill|Ryōmō Line|en|Ryōmō Line|WD=Q253391|lt=Ryōmō Line}}. A Fujisan Shrine (dedicated to the deity of Mount Fuji) sits atop the posterior section, from which the kofun derives its name. burial goods suggest the mound was constructed in the mid-5th century. Its burial facility features a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a hallmark of royal tombs from the Kinki region during the same period. The site was Historic Site1 a city historic site in 1966 and the sarcophagus was classified as a Prefectural Important designation1 in 2001. == burial == The area of the kofun are as followsarea based on the explanation board at the site.: * Total length: 125 meters * '''Anterior section''' ''' Width: 83.2 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters ''' Some sections removed for the Ryōmō Line construction. * '''Posterior section''' ''' Diameter: 77.2 meters ''' Height: 9.5 meters The mound's orientation is approximately 74° east of north. It features a three-tiered structure covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and lined with large cylindrical haniwa. Surrounding the mound is a shield-shaped moat, which extends the total length of the site to 192 meters. == burial goods == === Nagamochi-style Sarcophagus === [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun sekkan.JPG|thumb|240px|right|{{center|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important designation1)}}]] [[File:お富士山古墳石棺（復元）.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Replica of the sarcophagus}}{{small|Displayed at the National Museum of Japanese History.}}]] The [[Chest-shaped Sarcophagus|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus]] from Ofujiyama Kofun is preserved near the shrine at the posterior section. It is composed of six sandstone slabs: * Total length: 285 cm * Width: 121 cm * Weight: 6.8 tons The sarcophagus features a rectangular base typeed by a floor slab, two side slabs, and two end slabs. A rounded, domed lid rests on top. This sarcophagus type is characteristic of royal tombs in the Kinki region during the 5th century. In Gunma Prefecture, only the [[Ōta Tenjinyama Kofun|Ota Tenjinyama Kofun]] shares this feature. Few examples are found elsewhere in the Kanto regionOther examples include Takayanagi Chōshizuka Kofun and San-no-Funeme Ōtsukayama Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.. The craftsmanship and materials suggest that artisans from the Kinki region constructed the sarcophagus locally, underscoring strong ties with the Kinki region. Historical records such as the Edo-period "Hatsuhunreki" and "Isesaki Fudoki" also document the sarcophagus. A replica is displayed at the Gunma Prefectural Museum of History. === Other burial goods === burial goods include imitation stone knives made from steatite. File:お富士山古墳 Excavated Artifacts.JPG|burial goods
{{small|Exhibited at the Isesaki City Akabori History and Folklore Museum.}} == Historic Site == === Gunma Prefecture === * '''Important designation1 (Tangible designation1)''' ''' Nagamochi-style sarcophagus from Ofujiyama Kofun (archaeological material) – Designated March 23, 2001. === Isesaki City === * '''Historic Site''' ''' Ofujiyama Kofun – Designated April 12, 1966. == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Explanation Board at the Site (Isesaki City Board of Education, installed February 5, 1999) * Sarcophagus Preservation Facility Documentation == Related Sites == * {{ill|Keno Province|en|Keno Province|WD=Q1886428|lt=Keno}} * [[Ōta Tenjinyama Kofun|Ota Tenjinyama Kofun]] – The largest kofun in eastern Japan, also featuring a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Ofujiyama Kofun}} * [https://www.city.isesaki.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/shitei/shiseki/3380.html Ofujiyama Kofun] (Isesaki City Website) * [http://www.gunmaibun.org/remain/guide/tyumo/ohujiyama.html Ofujiyama Kofun] (Gunma Archaeological Research Center) {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|お富士山古墳|version=101597472|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:お富士山古墳]] {{Commons category|Ofujiyama Kofun}} {{wikidata link|Q17228034}} [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:History of Isesaki, Gunma]] [[Category:Isesaki, Gunma]] [[Category:Kofun of Gunma prefecture]] [[Category:Municipal Historic Sites in Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:pages]]

{{small|Exhibited at the Isesaki City Akabori History and Folklore Museum.}} == Historic Site == === Gunma Prefecture === * '''Important designation1 (Tangible designation1)''' ''' Nagamochi-style sarcophagus from Ofujiyama Kofun (archaeological material) – Designated March 23, 2001. === Isesaki City === * '''Historic Site''' ''' Ofujiyama Kofun – Designated April 12, 1966. == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Explanation Board at the Site (Isesaki City Board of Education, installed February 5, 1999) * Sarcophagus Preservation Facility Documentation == Related Sites == * {{ill|Keno Province|en|Keno Province|WD=Q1886428|lt=Keno|1=Keno Province}} * [[Ōta Tenjinyama Kofun|Ota Tenjinyama Kofun]] – The largest kofun in eastern Japan, also featuring a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Ofujiyama Kofun}} * [https://www.city.isesaki.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/shitei/shiseki/3380.html Ofujiyama Kofun] (Isesaki City Website) * [http://www.gunmaibun.org/remain/guide/tyumo/ohujiyama.html Ofujiyama Kofun] (Gunma Archaeological Research Center) {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|お富士山古墳|version=101597472|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:お富士山古墳]] {{Commons category|Ofujiyama Kofun}} {{wikidata link|Q17228034}} [[Category:History of Isesaki, Gunma]] [[Category:Isesaki, Gunma]] [[Category:Kofun of Gunma prefecture]] [[Category:Municipal Historic Sites in Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:pages]]

[[co:commons:Category:Ofujiyama Kofun]][[ja:お富士山古墳]] [[co:commons:Category:Ofujiyama Kofun]][[ja:お富士山古墳]] [[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Ofujiyama Kofun]][[ja:お富士山古墳]] {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] |name=Ofujiyama Kofun |location=[[:en:Isesaki, Gunma|Isesaki]], {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture|1=Gunma Prefecture}} |coordinates={{Coord|36.338036|N|139.172111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |type=[[Keyhole-shaped Kofun|Keyhole-shaped]] |built=Mid-5th century |burial=Nagamochi-style sarcophagus |length=125 meters |heritage=Isesaki City Designated Historic Site |notes=Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important designation1) |burial goods=[[Cylindrical Haniwa|Cylindrical haniwa]] |image=File:Ofujiyama-kofun zenkei-1.JPG |architectural_styles={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpōkōenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} }} [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun zenkei-1.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Panoramic view from the bridge}}{{small|Left: anterior section; right: posterior section.}}]] [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun funcho.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Posterior section}}{{small|Fujisan Shrine and sarcophagus (far right).}}]] '''Ofujiyama Kofun''' (お富士山古墳), also referred to as御富士山古墳, is a keyhole-shaped Kofun located in [[:en:Isesaki, Gunma|Isesaki City]], {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture|1=Gunma Prefecture}}. It has been Historic Site1 as a historic site by Isesaki City, and its Nagamochi-style sarcophagus is recognized as a Prefectural Important designation1. The mound, built during the mid-5th century, is notable for its use of a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a characteristic feature of royal tombs in the Kinki region. == Overview == Situated in the northwestern part of Isesaki City, Ofujiyama Kofun is the largest keyhole-shaped kofun in the city. The anterior section faces west-southwest, but part of it was removed to accommodate the construction of the {{ill|Ryōmō Line|en|Ryōmō Line|WD=Q253391|lt=Ryōmō Line|1=Ryōmō Line}}. A Fujisan Shrine (dedicated to the deity of Mount Fuji) sits atop the posterior section, from which the kofun derives its name. burial goods suggest the mound was constructed in the mid-5th century. Its burial facility features a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a hallmark of royal tombs from the Kinki region during the same period. The site was Historic Site1 a city historic site in 1966 and the sarcophagus was classified as a Prefectural Important designation1 in 2001. == burial == The area of the kofun are as followsarea based on the explanation board at the site.: * Total length: 125 meters * '''Anterior section''' ''' Width: 83.2 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters ''' Some sections removed for the Ryōmō Line construction. * '''Posterior section''' ''' Diameter: 77.2 meters ''' Height: 9.5 meters The mound's orientation is approximately 74° east of north. It features a three-tiered structure covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and lined with large cylindrical haniwa. Surrounding the mound is a shield-shaped moat, which extends the total length of the site to 192 meters. == burial goods == === Nagamochi-style Sarcophagus === [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun sekkan.JPG|thumb|240px|right|{{center|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important designation1)}}]] [[File:お富士山古墳石棺（復元）.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Replica of the sarcophagus}}{{small|Displayed at the National Museum of Japanese History.}}]] The [[Chest-shaped Sarcophagus|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus]] from Ofujiyama Kofun is preserved near the shrine at the posterior section. It is composed of six sandstone slabs: * Total length: 285 cm * Width: 121 cm * Weight: 6.8 tons The sarcophagus features a rectangular base typeed by a floor slab, two side slabs, and two end slabs. A rounded, domed lid rests on top. This sarcophagus type is characteristic of royal tombs in the Kinki region during the 5th century. In Gunma Prefecture, only the [[Ōta Tenjinyama Kofun|Ota Tenjinyama Kofun]] shares this feature. Few examples are found elsewhere in the Kanto regionOther examples include Takayanagi Chōshizuka Kofun and San-no-Funeme Ōtsukayama Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.. The craftsmanship and materials suggest that artisans from the Kinki region constructed the sarcophagus locally, underscoring strong ties with the Kinki region. Historical records such as the Edo-period "Hatsuhunreki" and "Isesaki Fudoki" also document the sarcophagus. A replica is displayed at the Gunma Prefectural Museum of History. === Other burial goods === burial goods include imitation stone knives made from steatite. File:お富士山古墳 Excavated Artifacts.JPG|burial goods

A rounded, domed lid rests on top. This sarcophagus type is characteristic of royal tombs in the Kinki region during the 5th century. Few examples are found elsewhere in the Kanto regionOther examples include Takayanagi Chōshizuka Kofun and San-no-Funeme Ōtsukayama Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.. The craftsmanship and materials suggest that artisans from the Kinki region constructed the sarcophagus locally, underscoring strong ties with the Kinki region. Historical records such as the Edo-period "Hatsuhunreki" and "Isesaki Fudoki" also document the sarcophagus. A replica is displayed at the Gunma Prefectural Museum of History. === Other burial goods === burial goods include imitation stone knives made from steatite. File:お富士山古墳 Excavated Artifacts.JPG|burial goods
{{small|Exhibited at the Isesaki City Akabori History and Folklore Museum.}} == Historic Site == === Gunma Prefecture === * '''Important designation1 (Tangible designation1)''' ''' Nagamochi-style sarcophagus from Ofujiyama Kofun (archaeological material) – Designated March 23, 2001. === Isesaki City === * '''Historic Site''' ''' Ofujiyama Kofun – Designated April 12, 1966. == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Explanation Board at the Site (Isesaki City Board of Education, installed February 5, 1999) * Sarcophagus Preservation Facility Documentation == Related Sites == * {{ill|Keno Province|en|Keno Province|WD=Q1886428|lt=Keno|1=Keno Province}} * [[Ōta Tenjinyama Kofun|Ota Tenjinyama Kofun]] – The largest kofun in eastern Japan, also featuring a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Ofujiyama Kofun}} * [https://www.city.isesaki.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/shitei/shiseki/3380.html Ofujiyama Kofun] (Isesaki City Website) * [http://www.gunmaibun.org/remain/guide/tyumo/ohujiyama.html Ofujiyama Kofun] (Gunma Archaeological Research Center) {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|お富士山古墳|version=101597472|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:お富士山古墳]] {{Commons category|Ofujiyama Kofun}} {{wikidata link|Q17228034}} [[Category:History of Isesaki, Gunma]] [[Category:Isesaki, Gunma]] [[Category:Kofun of Gunma prefecture]] [[Category:Municipal Historic Sites in Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Zenpō-kōen-fun]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpō-kōen-fun]] |name=Ofujiyama Kofun |location=[[:en:Isesaki, Gunma|Isesaki]], {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture|1=Gunma Prefecture}} |coordinates={{Coord|36.338036|N|139.172111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |type=[[Keyhole-shaped Kofun|Keyhole-shaped]] |built=Mid-5th century |burial=Nagamochi-style sarcophagus |length=125 meters |heritage=Isesaki City Designated Historic Site |notes=Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important designation1) |burial goods=[[Cylindrical Haniwa|Cylindrical haniwa]] |image=File:Ofujiyama-kofun zenkei-1.JPG |architectural_styles={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpō-kōen-fun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} }} [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun zenkei-1.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Panoramic view from the bridge}}{{small|Left: anterior section; right: posterior section.}}]] [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun funcho.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Posterior section}}{{small|Fujisan Shrine and sarcophagus (far right).}}]] '''Ofujiyama Kofun''' (お富士山古墳), also referred to as御富士山古墳, is a keyhole-shaped Kofun located in [[:en:Isesaki, Gunma|Isesaki City]], {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture|1=Gunma Prefecture}}. It has been Historic Site1 as a historic site by Isesaki City, and its Nagamochi-style sarcophagus is recognized as a Prefectural Important designation1. The mound, built during the mid-5th century, is notable for its use of a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a characteristic feature of royal tombs in the Kinki region. == Overview == Situated in the northwestern part of Isesaki City, Ofujiyama Kofun is the largest keyhole-shaped kofun in the city. The anterior section faces west-southwest, but part of it was removed to accommodate the construction of the {{ill|Ryōmō Line|en|Ryōmō Line|WD=Q253391|lt=Ryōmō Line|1=Ryōmō Line}}. A Fujisan Shrine (dedicated to the deity of Mount Fuji) sits atop the posterior section, from which the kofun derives its name. burial goods suggest the mound was constructed in the mid-5th century. Its burial facility features a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus, a hallmark of royal tombs from the Kinki region during the same period. The site was Historic Site1 a city historic site in 1966 and the sarcophagus was classified as a Prefectural Important designation1 in 2001. == burial == The area of the kofun are as followsarea based on the explanation board at the site.: * Total length: 125 meters * '''Anterior section''' ''' Width: 83.2 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters ''' Some sections removed for the Ryōmō Line construction. * '''Posterior section''' ''' Diameter: 77.2 meters ''' Height: 9.5 meters The mound's orientation is approximately 74° east of north. It features a three-tiered structure covered with fukiishi (paving stones) and lined with large cylindrical haniwa. Surrounding the mound is a shield-shaped moat, which extends the total length of the site to 192 meters. == burial goods == === Nagamochi-style Sarcophagus === [[File:Ofujiyama-kofun sekkan.JPG|thumb|240px|right|{{center|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus (Prefectural Important designation1)}}]] [[File:お富士山古墳石棺（復元）.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Replica of the sarcophagus}}{{small|Displayed at the National Museum of Japanese History.}}]] The [[Chest-shaped Sarcophagus|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus]] from Ofujiyama Kofun is preserved near the shrine at the posterior section. It is composed of six sandstone slabs: * Total length: 285 cm * Width: 121 cm * Weight: 6.8 tons The sarcophagus features a rectangular base typeed by a floor slab, two side slabs, and two end slabs. A rounded, domed lid rests on top. This sarcophagus type is characteristic of royal tombs in the Kinki region during the 5th century. In Gunma Prefecture, only the [[Ōta Tenjinyama Kofun|Ota Tenjinyama Kofun]] shares this feature. Few examples are found elsewhere in the Kanto regionOther examples include Takayanagi Chōshizuka Kofun and San-no-Funeme Ōtsukayama Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.. The craftsmanship and materials suggest that artisans from the Kinki region constructed the sarcophagus locally, underscoring strong ties with the Kinki region. Historical records such as the Edo-period "Hatsuhunreki" and "Isesaki Fudoki" also document the sarcophagus. A replica is displayed at the Gunma Prefectural Museum of History. === Other burial goods === burial goods include imitation stone knives made from steatite. File:お富士山古墳 Excavated Artifacts.JPG|burial goods
{{small|Exhibited at the Isesaki City Akabori History and Folklore Museum.}} == Historic Site == === Gunma Prefecture === * '''Important designation1 (Tangible designation1)''' ''' Nagamochi-style sarcophagus from Ofujiyama Kofun (archaeological material) – Designated March 23, 2001. === Isesaki City === * '''Historic Site''' ''' Ofujiyama Kofun – Designated April 12, 1966. == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Explanation Board at the Site (Isesaki City Board of Education, installed February 5, 1999) * Sarcophagus Preservation Facility Documentation == Related Sites == * {{ill|Keno Province|en|Keno Province|WD=Q1886428|lt=Keno|1=Keno Province}} * [[Ōta Tenjinyama Kofun|Ota Tenjinyama Kofun]] – The largest kofun in eastern Japan, also featuring a Nagamochi-style sarcophagus. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Ofujiyama Kofun}} * [https://www.city.isesaki.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/shitei/shiseki/3380.html Ofujiyama Kofun] (Isesaki City Website) * [http://www.gunmaibun.org/remain/guide/tyumo/ohujiyama.html Ofujiyama Kofun] (Gunma Archaeological Research Center) {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|お富士山古墳|version=101597472|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{Commons category|Ofujiyama Kofun}} {{wikidata link|Q17228034|co|commons:Category:Ofujiyama Kofun|ja|お富士山古墳}} [[Category:History of Isesaki, Gunma]] [[Category:Isesaki, Gunma]] [[Category:Kofun of Gunma prefecture]] [[Category:Municipal Historic Sites in Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Zenpō-kōen-fun]]
